title: OpenAI debuts ChatGPT Work, an agentic tool for automating business workflows
blurb: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, an agentic mode powered by its new GPT-5.6 frontier model that decomposes a goal into subtasks and executes them across connected apps, files, and desktops over extended timeframes. It ships with plugins for Slack, Teams, Gmail, Google Drive, Salesforce, and SharePoint invoked via "@", folds in the full Codex experience, and pauses for human approval before sensitive actions.

title: Meta jumps into AI coding market in effort to chase Anthropic and OpenAI
blurb: Meta released Muse Spark 1.1, which AI chief Alexandr Wang called its strongest model for agentic and coding work yet, opening API access through a public developer preview. The model undercuts rivals on price at $1.25 per million input tokens and $4.25 per million output tokens, with $20 in free credits per account, though access is limited to Meta's own properties for now.

title: OpenAI launches ChatGPT Work and unveils unified desktop app with Codex built in
blurb: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work and consolidated its tools into a single desktop app for Mac and Windows, with Codex now built directly into ChatGPT. The move retires the standalone Codex app and discontinues the Atlas browser, folding coding and agent workflows into one application rather than a set of separate products.

title: TypeProbe: Recovering Type Representations from Hidden States of Pre-trained Code Models
blurb: TypeProbe probes the residual streams of pretrained code models and finds that cross-lingual type representations emerge even from untyped code. Using parallel Java and Python datasets, the authors show that probes trained on one language can infer argument and result types in the other, and that this structure holds up under lexical perturbations and syntactic variation. The code and datasets are released.

title: Meta launches low-cost Muse Spark 1.1 as enterprise AI spending comes under scrutiny
blurb: Meta released Muse Spark 1.1, a frontier model in public preview on the Meta Model API that matches or competes with Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5 on coding, agentic, and computer-use benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified and OSWorld. At $1.25 per million input tokens and $4.25 per million output tokens, it undercuts GPT-5.5 output pricing by about 86% and Claude Opus 4.8 by over 90%.

title: Grounded Event Extraction from SEC 8-K Filings with a Fine-Grained Taxonomy
blurb: This paper presents a two-stage system that tags SEC 8-K disclosures against a three-tier taxonomy of 119 event types, anchoring every label to a verbatim quote via fuzzy n-gram validation and re-grading each citation for quality in a dedicated second pass. Applied to 292,984 filings from 2022 to 2026, it produced 601,088 grounded event tags, now released, with precision rising monotonically from 12% to 96% as the quality score increases.

title: UniClawBench: A Universal Benchmark for Proactive Agents on Real-World Tasks
blurb: UniClawBench is a capability-driven benchmark that evaluates proactive LLM agents on 400 bilingual real-world tasks inside live Docker containers, scoring them against fine-grained step-by-step checkpoints rather than static pre-recorded answers. It organizes tasks around five capabilities (Skill Usage, Exploration, Long-Context Reasoning, Multimodal Understanding, and Cross-Platform Coordination) and uses a closed-loop executor, hidden supervisor, and user agent to simulate multi-turn feedback without leaking grading criteria.

title: Building a real-time AI tutor for 5-year-olds
blurb: This post details the architecture behind Ello's real-time AI tutor for children ages 4-9, which requires sub-second responses on every turn because a two-second pause is enough to lose a young child's attention. The team abandoned the standard agent tool loop, which adds 3-4 seconds of latency per turn from frontier models' 2-3 second time-to-first-token, in favor of a design that grounds pedagogy in the engineering itself.

title: AI Gateways Offer Attackers the Keys to the Kingdom
blurb: Darktrace researchers investigated an incident where an attacker compromised an externally exposed AWS EC2 instance hosting an AI gateway connected to Amazon Bedrock, using the access for cryptomining. The same foothold could have exposed connected foundation models, proprietary data, and IAM credentials, making centralized AI gateways an attractive aggregation point for credential theft, data access, and cloud persistence.

title: WebSwarm: Recursive Multi-Agent Orchestration for Deep-and-Wide Web Search
blurb: WebSwarm is a recursive multi-agent framework for deep-and-wide web search that dynamically spawns search nodes, each pairing a local objective with a search mode and able to either solve its task or delegate to child nodes that return evidence upward. On BrowseComp-Plus, WideSearch, DeepWideSearch, and GISA, it outperforms single-agent and multi-agent baselines across deep, wide, and interleaved tasks.

title: Remember When It Matters: Proactive Memory Agent for Long-Horizon Agents
blurb: This paper introduces a proactive memory agent that runs alongside an unmodified action agent, maintaining a structured memory bank and deciding when to inject a grounded reminder or stay silent to counter "behavioral state decay" in long-horizon tasks. The plug-and-play module improved pass@1 by 8.3 points on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and 6.8 points on τ²-Bench, with selective intervention beating always-on injection, advisor-only guidance, and general retrieval.

title: Token-Flow Firewall: Semantic Runtime Auditing for Persistent AI Agents
blurb: TokenWall is a runtime defense framework that audits the natural-language token flows between a persistent AI agent's memory, tools, and retrieved files, intercepting risky semantic flows before they reach privileged execution sinks. On CIK-Bench, it cut attack success rate to 12.5% while keeping a 97.4% benign pass rate and adding just 0.69 seconds of latency per benign case.

title: MiniMax Joins China AI Fundraising Rush With $2 Billion Deal
blurb: MiniMax is seeking to raise as much as $2 billion through a sale of new shares and convertible bonds to fund its AI buildout. The round adds MiniMax to a wave of Chinese technology companies tapping capital markets to finance the infrastructure and model development behind their AI ambitions.

title: Tool-Making and Self-Evolving LLM Agents in Low-Latency Systems
blurb: This paper replaces the inference-time coding loop in production LLM agents with a tool-making pipeline that compiles repeated SOP steps into validated, versioned tools before deployment, grounding synthesis in live execution traces and backend schemas. Deployed in a Fulfillment Center alarm-triage system over a 44-node SOP, the direct tool calls cut p50 latency by 42% and reduced end-to-end error rate by up to 53% across 1,500 historical alarms.

title: Multi-Agent Firewall Architecture for Privacy Protection of Sensitive Data in Interactions with Language Models
blurb: This paper proposes an open-source, privacy-focused firewall that intercepts both web-based and programmatic LLM traffic across HTTP(S) and WebSocket connections using a browser extension and proxy. A multi-agent pipeline combines deterministic detectors with LLM-driven semantic analysis to prevent data and proprietary code leakage, reaching F1 scores of up to 94.93% on optimal configurations.

title: Feedback Manipulation Regularization: Enabling Offline Agent Alignment for Imitation Learning
blurb: This paper introduces Feedback Manipulation Regularization (FMR), an algorithm-agnostic method that uses evaluative human feedback as a corrective signal to align imitation learning policies in a single-stage offline training setup. Tested on adapted Safety Gymnasium environments, FMR cut misalignment by up to 98% across multiple imitation learning algorithms and stayed robust with scarce aligned data and noisy demonstrations.

title: Meta Launches New A.I. Model as Global Technology Race Heats Up
blurb: Meta released a new AI model and, for the first time, will offer a paid version of the service. The move marks a departure from the company's longtime philosophy of giving its AI away for free, coming as competition among global technology firms intensifies.

title: Open-source AI developer tool Ollama raises $65M to grow its platform
blurb: Ollama raised $65M in a Series B round led by Theory Ventures, bringing its total funding to $88M. The tool, used by 8.9 million developers monthly and 85% of the Fortune 500, lets developers run open models locally or swap to cloud inference through the same OpenAI-compatible API and single command.

title: SLORR: Simple and Efficient In-Training Low-Rank Regularization
blurb: SLORR is a stateless, architecture-preserving framework for in-training low-rank regularization that makes neural networks more compressible without SVDs, extra trainable parameters, or cached state. It uses GPU-friendly approximations of the Hoyer sparsity metric and nuclear norm, adding under 8% training overhead on ImageNet-1K models and under 1% on 135M and 560M LLM pretraining while preserving accuracy after compression.

title: UltraX: Refining Pre-Training Data at Scale with Adaptive Programmatic Editing
blurb: UltraX is a function-calling framework for refining large-scale LLM pre-training data through instance-level programmatic editing, adding insertion to the usual deletion and modification operations. It generates program supervision from expert-LLM-refined text pairs, validates outputs with sliding-window prediction and operation aggregation, and matches or beats baselines while using fewer training tokens.

title: From Prompts to Contracts: Harness Engineering for Auditable Enterprise LLM Agents
blurb: This paper presents harness engineering, an approach that moves deterministic behavior into code, manifests, schemas, and validation artifacts around a replaceable model composition boundary while keeping source-backed claims as the runtime authority. Tested on a public-data slice of 25 Korean listed companies across three hosted models, code-owned enforcement blocked trace-leakage and recommendation-language violations that prompting alone let through, preserving full 120/120 utility where a bolt-on guardrail over-refused to 88/120.

title: Stop Guessing When to Stop Testing: Efficient Model Evaluation with Just Enough Data
blurb: This paper proposes replacing fixed-size benchmarks with sequential testing, an adaptive evaluation framework that stops collecting data once a target like diminishing returns or minimum detectable effect size is met. On the Open VLM Leaderboard, the method cut computational cost by 80% versus fixed-size evaluation at a 2.5-point confidence-interval width while preserving statistical significance.

title: Own the Outer Loop
blurb: Addy Osmani argues that as agentic engineering shifts toward harnesses, loops, and software factories, engineers should own the outer loop rather than the inner one. The inner loop of writing individual lines of code is increasingly automated, but accountability for architecture, verification, and shipping outcomes cannot be delegated to agents. His point is that engineering value moves up the stack to orchestration and judgment, not away from responsibility.

title: ChatGPT Work
blurb: OpenAI released ChatGPT Work, an agent that gathers information across a user's connected apps and files to produce finished sheets, slides, docs, and web apps, breaking complex projects into steps it completes independently over hours. It runs on Codex technology and GPT-5.6, OpenAI's latest frontier model rolling out the same day, and can carry context through multi-step workflows and keep tasks moving via Scheduled Tasks.

title: Show HN: FableCut – A browser video editor AI agents can drive (zero deps)
blurb: FableCut is a Premiere-style non-linear video editor that runs entirely in the browser and exposes its full timeline as a single project.json document that any process speaking MCP or REST can edit. The project file is the interface, so a human and an AI agent can cut the same timeline simultaneously while the browser UI hot-reloads within ~150ms via server-sent events, with zero npm dependencies and one node server.

title: How Deutsche Telekom is rewiring telecommunications with AI
blurb: Deutsche Telekom, which serves over 300 million customers and employs more than 200,000 people, is deploying ChatGPT Enterprise and API tooling across its workforce in a bid to become one of the first AI-native telcos. The company reports over 50,000 monthly active users and a 546% jump in AI tool usage since the start of 2026, with early investment concentrated in customer care and network operations.

title: An AI agent startup just let its agent run its $100M fundraise
blurb: Lyzr used its own AI agent, SivaClaw, to run its $100M Series B at a ~$500M valuation, fielding questions from over 130 investors.

title: OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, but its AI browser ambitions are still growing
blurb: OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, the AI browser it launched in October, and redistributing its agentic browsing features across the ChatGPT desktop app and a new Google Chrome extension. The Chrome extension reads the context of the current page and competes directly with Google's Gemini Side Panel, while the desktop app gains a built-in browser and a server-side cloud browser for running agent tasks remotely.

title: Meta enters the crowded AI coding battle with Muse Spark 1.1
blurb: Meta released Muse Spark 1.1, a multimodal model for agentic coding that competes with OpenAI and Anthropic on multistep reasoning, bug fixing, and large code migrations. Meta priced it at $1.25 per million input tokens and $4.25 per million output tokens, roughly in line with Claude Haiku 4.5 and GPT-5.6 Luna, and Zuckerberg signaled more models are coming.

title: New York Times says OpenAI hid evidence in ChatGPT copyright trial
blurb: The New York Times and The Daily News accused OpenAI of concealing evidence in their two-year copyright suit, alleging a court-ordered deposition showed OpenAI had already searched its training corpus for their journalism despite claiming it could not. The filing also alleges OpenAI amassed 78 million de-identified ChatGPT conversations to measure its own infringement and deleted billions of outputs in violation of a preservation order.

title: The ChatGPT browser is already dead
blurb: OpenAI will shut down ChatGPT Atlas, its agentic browser, on August 9th, less than a year after announcing it in October. The move is part of the ChatGPT Work launch, which folds Atlas's browsing and agent capabilities into an updated desktop browser and a cloud browser, as OpenAI cuts "side quests" to focus on productivity features.

title: Meta’s new AI chips will begin production in September
blurb: Meta will begin production of the latest chips from its Meta Training and Inference Accelerator (MTIA) program in September, working with Broadcom on design and TSMC on manufacturing. The chips are aimed at training its ranking and recommendation models and running inference for its apps, reducing but not eliminating Meta's reliance on Nvidia and AMD GPUs as capex climbs to $125B-$145B this year.

title: OpenAI may have made a fatal misstep in copyright fight with news orgs
blurb: News organizations suing OpenAI, led by The New York Times, filed a sanctions motion accusing the company of concealing evidence for two years by falsely claiming it lacked the technical ability to search ChatGPT logs for paywall-skirting output. The logs are central to the case, potentially either establishing infringement or supporting OpenAI's fair use defense, and a re-deposed engineer's testimony reportedly exposed the concealment.

title: OpenAI wants its new tool to do your work for you and with you
blurb: OpenAI released ChatGPT Work, an agent that can stay on a project for hours to turn a goal into finished output while pausing to get user approval on important actions. The tool integrates Scheduled Tasks for recurring cron-style jobs, connects to Slack, Microsoft Teams, Google Drive, and SharePoint, and can modify desktop files, as OpenAI sunsets its Atlas browser less than nine months after launch.

title: Carlyle agrees to sell US data center power and infrastructure platform Copia to EQT; sources: the deal values Copia at $2.6B, a fivefold return for Carlyle (Financial Times)
blurb: Carlyle agreed to sell Copia, its US data center power and infrastructure platform, to EQT in a deal that values Copia at $2.6B. Sources say the sale represents a fivefold return for Carlyle, underscoring investor appetite for the power and physical infrastructure underpinning AI data center growth.

title: China Omits Job Goal for First Time in Decades as AI Spreads
blurb: China omitted a numerical target for urban job creation from its next five-year plan for the first time in decades, citing rising uncertainty over employment as AI spreads through the economy. The move signals Beijing's reluctance to commit to hiring goals it may not meet as automation reshapes labor demand across sectors.

title: OpenAI's newest AI model is 54% more token efficient on agentic coding, Altman tells CNBC
blurb: OpenAI began rolling out its GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models, which Sam Altman says are 54% more token efficient on agentic coding tasks and "as good or better" than competing models. The initial release was limited to a small group of trusted partners at the request of the US government, following a safety approval process OpenAI worked through with Commerce, Treasury, and cyber officials.

title: Memory chip giant SK hynix bags $26.5B in blockbuster US listing
blurb: SK hynix priced its American Depositary Receipts at $149 to raise roughly $26.5B ahead of its Nasdaq debut under the "SKHY" ticker, making it the second-largest share sale in history behind SpaceX. The world's top supplier of high-bandwidth memory chips saw demand exceed available shares sevenfold, and it will use the proceeds to fund new chipmaking facilities and equipment.

title: MiniMax Shares Drop on $2 Billion Fundraising Plan
blurb: MiniMax shares fell after the Chinese AI company disclosed a plan to raise $2B through a share placement and convertible-bond offering. The dilution from the combined equity and debt raise pressured the stock, even as the capital targets the rising compute and model-training costs facing Chinese AI developers.

title: Shared API keys expose AI agents at 69% of enterprises, new VentureBeat research finds
blurb: VentureBeat's June 2026 Pulse Research found that 69% of enterprises run AI agents that share API keys across multiple workflows, meaning one compromised agent inherits the accumulated permissions of every agent on that credential. The finding coincides with more than $22 billion in identity-security acquisitions over the past year, including Palo Alto Networks buying CyberArk for $21.1 billion and CrowdStrike acquiring runtime authorization platform SGNL.

title: Meta launches flagship Muse Spark 1.1 model with multi-agent upgrades
blurb: Meta released Muse Spark 1.1, a flagship LLM optimized for multi-agent automation, available in the Meta AI chatbot and through the new Meta Model API in public preview. The model pairs a 1M-token context window with a context compaction mechanism that preserves key details across sub-tasks, and scored 72.2 on Vibe Code Bench v1.1, over 50 points ahead of Meta's prior flagship.

title: AI agent startup Lyzr reportedly raising $100M at $500M valuation
blurb: Lyzr, a two-year-old startup whose cloud platform lets developers build enterprise AI agents in minutes, is reportedly raising roughly $100M at a $500M valuation, double its March worth. Bloomberg reports the round has drawn $400M in investor interest from Silicon Valley funds, Middle Eastern VCs, and financial institutions, and Lyzr claims its platform cuts agent deployment work by over 70%.

title: OpenAI is killing its ChatGPT Atlas browser less than a year after launch
blurb: OpenAI is shutting down its ChatGPT Atlas browser on August 9, less than a year after its October 2025 launch, and folding its capabilities into a new desktop app called ChatGPT Work. The consolidated app merges ChatGPT, Codex, and Atlas into one experience with a built-in browser, a separate cloud browser for remote agent tasks, tabs, a password manager, and autofill.

title: News outlets ask a judge to sanction OpenAI in their landmark copyright fight
blurb: A group of news publishers led by The New York Times asked a Manhattan federal judge to sanction OpenAI, alleging it concealed and destroyed training datasets and ChatGPT logs central to their copyright case. The stakes are high after Anthropic agreed to pay book authors $1.5B, roughly $3,000 per work, and the Times says it has already spent over $28M fighting AI companies.

title: Prime Intellect raises $130M at $1B valuation for its AI training platform
blurb: Prime Intellect raised $130M at a $1B valuation from investors including Nvidia's NVentures, Intel Capital, and Dell Technologies Capital to expand its cloud platform for customizing open-source AI models. The platform pairs its Verifiers and Prime-RL toolkits to build reinforcement-learning environments and parallelize training runs across thousands of GPUs, with support for both full fine-tuning and LoRA.

title: AWS gives its ERP agent deny-by-default rules and a separate identity
blurb: AWS released the Agentic AI Solutions Framework for SAP, which hands ERP exception work like invoice matching and payment reconciliation to a single agent running on Amazon Bedrock AgentCore and its open source Strands SDK. The agent operates deny-by-default with its own identity, reads plain-language SOPs to resolve exceptions, and progresses from advisory mode to autonomous execution only as confidence crosses defined thresholds.

title: Report: Apple interested in startup that runs giant AI models on iPhone without servers
blurb: The Information reports that Apple has held meetings with PrismML, a startup that shrinks server-scale AI models to run on-device, and is exploring ways to use its technology. PrismML says it compressed Alibaba's 27B-parameter Qwen 3.6 to run on an iPhone 17 Pro, far above the few billion active parameters typical for phones, and plans to release the open-source model on July 14.

title: Fabless South Korean startup wants to entice European inference chip-hungry enterprises
blurb: FuriosaAI, the South Korean chip startup that rejected Meta's $800M acquisition offer, is expanding into Europe through a deal to deploy its RNGD inference accelerators in Equinix's Lisbon data center. The 180-watt, PCIe-based accelerators are built on TSMC's 5nm process and pitched as a non-American compute option, with the Lisbon site and a new Portugal office acting as showrooms for European enterprises.

title: Analysis: Architect and the $11 Trillion AI Capital Markets
blurb: This analysis argues that the next AI frontier is financial infrastructure, not better models, as GPUs, compute capacity, and inference become tradable financial assets. It projects $11T in AI infrastructure investment between 2024 and 2029, roughly $7T of it debt-financed, creating demand for hedging and derivatives. The piece points to Architect's ComputeConnect, a regulated exchange linking GPU derivatives to physical compute delivery, as an early example of price discovery and risk management arriving for the largest tech investment cycle in history.

title: Ideas Have Genomes: Benchmarking Scientific Lineage Reasoning and Lineage-Grounded Idea Generation
blurb: This paper introduces IG-Bench, a benchmark that treats scientific ideas as inheritable "genomes" to test whether AI systems can trace lineage and generate proposals that fit as coherent descendants of prior work. It spans 1,961 lineage traces across 10 domains, and the strongest of 14 tested LLM scientists reached only 27.3% exact accuracy on lineage reasoning, exposing a compositional bottleneck.

title: ProjAgent: Procedural Similarity Retrieval for Repository-Level Code Generation
blurb: ProjAgent is a repository-level code generation system that adds procedural similarity as an explicit retrieval signal, decomposing a target function into reasoning steps and using an agentic workflow to retrieve functions with similar procedural behavior at each step. Combined with semantic retrieval and a static-analysis repair loop, it reaches 41.14% Pass@1 on REPOCOD, beating existing retrieval-based baselines.

title: AUTOPILOT VQA: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for Incident-Centric Dashcam Understanding
blurb: AUTOPILOT-VQA is an incident-centric visual question answering benchmark that tests whether vision-language models can reason about safety-critical events in dashcam video. It spans categories including weather and lighting, road layout, signage, accident occurrence, impact location, and avoidability reasoning, pushing evaluation beyond object recognition toward temporally grounded, safety-aware reasoning, and ships as part of the AUTOPILOT CVPR 2026 competition.

title: Mistral unveils Robostral model for robot navigation with RGB camera
blurb: Mistral released Robostral Navigate, an 8B-parameter embodied navigation model that guides wheeled, legged, and aerial robots through real-world environments using a single RGB camera and plain-language instructions. It hits a 76.6% success rate on the R2R-CE benchmark for unseen environments, beating the best single-camera approach by 9.7 points and leading multi-sensor systems by 4.5 points.

title: SolarChain-Eval: A Physics-Constrained Benchmark for Trustworthy Economic Agents in Decentralized Energy Markets
blurb: SolarChain-Eval is a physics-constrained benchmark that evaluates autonomous economic agents in decentralized energy markets, scoring policies on market utility, physical safety, slippage, fairness, and auditability through a Gymnasium-compatible MDP. Experiments across static, RL, and RL+LLM policies expose a utility-safety trade-off, showing that an LLM Planner/Auditor layer improves auditability but cannot compensate for a misspecified reward function.

title: The complexities of patient-centred conversational artificial intelligence
blurb: This paper analyzed 2,053 real patient-chatbot conversations and found that communication patterns and emotional expression vary widely, unlike the cooperative, articulate simulated patients most health chatbots are tested against. Across 1,164 clinician-graded cases spanning five patient personae, the authors show that communication style alone can significantly alter triage outcomes from four LLMs, risking underperformance and amplified health disparities in real-world use.

title: DocMaster: A Hierarchical Structure-Aware System for Document Analysis
blurb: DocMaster is a document analysis system that parses documents into hierarchical trees preserving sections, tables, figures, and equations rather than flattening them into plain-text chunks. It builds a structure-aware semantic index that supports natural-language filtering across large collections and follow-up question answering over the selected subset, with source code, data, and an interactive web demo available.

title: Formal Mechanisms for Market Stability in Self-Interested Agent Societies: A Marketplace Simulation Study
blurb: This paper tests which formal mechanisms keep a society of self-interested LLM agents trading cooperatively, using a marketplace of 18 DeepSeek-V3 agents that trade over 200 rounds under escalating troll injection. Mediation outperformed seven other conditions and held up under adversarial red-teaming, where the best optimized attack cut honest-agent utility by 13.3% but could not collapse the market.

title: Secure Decentralized Federated Learning via Gossip and Virtual Voting
blurb: This paper proposes gspDAG-FL, a decentralized federated learning framework that derives Byzantine-resilient consensus from the same peer-to-peer gossip history nodes use to share model updates. Full nodes reconstruct a compact topology DAG and run Hashgraph-style virtual voting to finalize model-origin tuples, matching ledger-based FL quality on MNIST and Penn Treebank across networks up to 100 nodes while cutting coordination overhead.

title: Structural Bottlenecks on Frequency Representation in End-to-End Audio Models
blurb: This paper shows that state-of-the-art strided convolutional audio encoders impose two structural bottlenecks on frequency representation, collapsing 31-35% of time-frequency primitives into alias equivalence classes and running filter bandwidths 10-35x above the theoretical resolution bound. The authors introduce Gabor Latent Refactorization, a retraining-free intervention that re-expresses encoder latents in a frequency-localized basis, cutting bandwidths to 1.5-3x while preserving reconstruction fidelity and improving pitch control.

title: Improving Ad-hoc Search Effectiveness for Conversational Information Retrieval via Model Merging
blurb: This paper introduces model merging as a training-free way to build a single retriever that handles both ad-hoc and conversational search without additional fine-tuning, sidestepping the retraining cost and catastrophic forgetting of standard approaches. Using linear and non-linear merging strategies (Model Soup and Slerp), the authors report up to 15% higher NDCG@3 under zero-shot conditions.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability of LLM Jailbreaks via Internal Attribution Graphs
blurb: This paper introduces a mechanistic framework that diagnoses LLM jailbreaks by aligning internal computation graphs from clean and attacked prompts, revealing that adversarial attacks systematically suppress safety-relevant components, spawn attack-specific features, and reroute computation paths. Across multiple open-source models and jailbreak benchmarks, structural deviations in these graphs correlated with unsafe behavior, and targeted interventions on the identified vulnerability motifs improved robustness.

title: Echoes Across Vietnam's Highlands, Delta, and Coast: A Multilingual Corpus for Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung
blurb: CKTN is the first corpus and benchmark for Vietnam's Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung languages, with 44,367 documents and 24M subword tokens.

title: Open Models, Open Risks: Measuring Unsafe Generation in Text-to-Image Models In the Wild
blurb: This paper presents a large-scale empirical study of jailbreak safety across 200+ text-to-image models pulled from Hugging Face, arguing that detector-only metrics overestimate real risk due to semantic drift and generation artifacts. The authors introduce Advanced ASR to measure semantically valid unsafe outputs, and find that safety degradation is neither universal nor uniform, though a subset of NSFW-oriented and seemingly benign models rank as high-risk.

title: How Analysts Use AI in High-Stakes Crime Linkage: An Industrial Study
blurb: A UK law enforcement agency co-developed an AI decision-support tool for crime linkage analysis, and this paper reports an industrial usability study of how specialist analysts actually use it. Combining direct observation, eye-tracking, mouse-tracking, and surveys, the authors found analysts used AI predictions selectively and repeatedly validated them against traditional behavioural evidence, reflecting partial trust and a need to better integrate model explanations into existing workflows.

title: PERFOPT-Bench: Evaluating Coding Agents on Software Performance Optimization
blurb: PERFOPT-Bench is a benchmark that evaluates whether coding agents can profile executions, fix cross-layer bottlenecks, and produce reproducible speedups on deliberately suboptimal codebases, scoring each task with hidden correctness tests, verified-speedup measurement, and trajectory audits. Across 7 agent stacks on 7 long-horizon tasks, optimization performance was workload-dependent rather than tied to model identity, and raw speedup proved unsafe as a score because some large gains came from benchmark-specific shortcut exploitation.

title: Launch HN: Context.dev (YC S26) – API to get structured data from any website
blurb: Context.dev (YC S26) launched an API that scrapes any website and returns structured data via JSON schema, plus brand and company data.

title: Behavior Foundations for Quadruped Robots: ABot-C0 Technical Report
blurb: ABot-C0 is a generalist motion-control system for quadruped robots built on three foundations: a multi-source motion-data pipeline, robust policy learning across tracking, locomotion, and scene interaction, and a unified real-world deployment stack. Trained on 16,074 physically feasible motion clips, its Flow-Matching policy shows a scaling law for quadruped motion tracking, with performance improving as training scales and zero-shot tracking of unseen motions.

title: OpenAI buys Northslope to put its engineers inside your business
blurb: OpenAI's Deployment Company agreed to acquire Northslope, an applied-AI firm, adding hundreds of forward-deployed engineers who embed inside client businesses to build AI systems around their operations. The deal is the unit's second in two months after Tomoro, drawing on a $4B acquisition fund and copying Palantir's embedded-engineer playbook, with terms undisclosed and regulatory clearance still pending.

title: New York Times and Other Publishers Ask Court to Penalize OpenAI
blurb: The New York Times, The New York Daily News, and other media organizations asked a court to sanction OpenAI, accusing the company of withholding evidence in their ongoing copyright lawsuit. The publishers allege OpenAI failed to preserve and produce relevant data, escalating a case that centers on the use of copyrighted news articles to train its models.

title: Cognitive-structured Multimodal Agent for Multimodal Understanding, Generation, and Editing
blurb: This paper introduces a Cognitive-structured Multimodal Agent that externalizes visual inputs into an Episodic Visual Memory and selectively retrieves relevant episodes, avoiding the visual token explosion that limits long-horizon multimodal dialogue. The 8B agent hits 91.4% retrieval accuracy over 20-turn sessions, beating 32B baselines by 8.2% while cutting per-turn inference time from 23.1s to 12.7s.

title: How did the government decide OpenAI’s frontier model was safe to release?
blurb: OpenAI is rolling out Sol, its latest frontier LLM, for wide public access, a model considered at least on par with Anthropic's Fable, which was briefly banned over capability and ownership concerns. Researchers, policy advisors, and lab employees told TechCrunch that no one understands the government's approval process, and an executive order tasks six cabinet agencies with defining one by early August.

title: MPFlow: Learning Budgeted Max-Flow Optimization on the Lightning Network with Deep Graph Reinforcement Learning
blurb: MPFlow casts liquidity placement on the Bitcoin Lightning Network as a budget-constrained combinatorial problem, using graph reinforcement learning to pick which k channels a node should open to maximize s-t max-flow routing capacity. Its message-passing PPO agent, trained with a hub-exclusion curriculum, beats heuristic baselines on real network snapshots and has run in production for 4,640 channel-open decisions allocating 267.3 BTC across 30 managed nodes.

title: Google launches LiteRT.js to make AI and ML workloads faster in the browser
blurb: Google released LiteRT.js, a browser-based runtime for running AI and ML models locally with GPU acceleration through WebGPU. The library targets client-side inference to cut latency and keep data on-device, giving web developers a path to run models without round-trips to a server.

title: Anthropic brings Claude Cowork to web and mobile for Max users
blurb: Anthropic expanded Claude Cowork to web and mobile in beta, starting with Max users, letting scheduled tasks run while a device is offline.

title: Ensemble Diversity Optimization for Subjective Supervision
blurb: This paper introduces Ensemble Diversity Optimization (EDO), a differentiable framework that jointly learns ensemble weights, size, and calibration to model annotator disagreement on subjective NLP tasks. Using a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation and a signed diversity regularizer, EDO cut cross-entropy by 40-78% and lowered Brier scores across four text-classification benchmarks while keeping F1 competitive with baselines like Top-5 Voting and WEL.

title: Functional and Secure Code Generation with Task Vectors
blurb: SecVecCoder is a method that uses task-vector arithmetic, linear operations on LLM weights, to make coding models generate functional code with fewer security vulnerabilities without any post-generation fixing. Across six coding LLMs on the CodeGuard+ benchmark, it raised trustworthy code completions by 2.1 to 36.0 percentage points over base models, with gains up to 39.1 points on unseen CWE types and decoding latency within 0.6% of the base.

title: VocaDet: Sample-Driven Open-Vocabulary Object Detection and Segmentation via Visual Tokenization and Vector Database Retrieval
blurb: VocaDet is a sample-driven framework for open-vocabulary object detection and segmentation that learns object concepts from user-provided positive and negative samples without any model retraining. It uses DINOv3 features and agglomerative clustering to convert images into discrete visual tokens stored in a vector database, then matches query images against those memories for retrieval-based localization and segmentation that expands as new samples accumulate.

title: AutoPersonas: A Multi-Timescale Loop Engine for Open-Ended Persona Evolution
blurb: This paper introduces AutoPersonas, an engine that combats "self-locking," a failure mode where long-running persona agents collapse into repetitive, stale lives as models converge on high-probability behaviors. Its OSO loop separates environment occurrences, observations, and persona state, and in a 40-day A/B test, context-slice masking plus per-sample divergence targeting cut macro-theme repetition from 61.8% to 36.3% while roughly doubling cumulative theme count.

title: AI changes the economics of software rewrites
blurb: The author argues AI has changed the economics of software rewrites, because model output quality depends less on prompting than on how much the codebase matches what the model already knows. Popular, consistent stacks get leverage from millions of training examples, while proprietary languages and inconsistent patterns must be taught through a limited context window, raising tokens, variance, and cost. A rewrite is therefore a chance to rebuild around clear patterns that play to AI's strengths rather than fighting them.

title: Palo Alto CEO Arora says AI pricing needs to fall 90% as token costs skyrocket
blurb: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora said AI token costs need to fall as much as 90% within two years to make large-scale enterprise adoption viable. He wants roughly 20% cuts over the next 12 months, and framed high pricing as a barrier already pushing businesses toward cheaper open-weight models, including Chinese ones closing the gap with US labs.

title: Mercor buys Deeptune to build training environments for AI agents
blurb: Mercor acquired Deeptune, a New York startup that builds reinforcement learning "training gyms" replicating hundreds of enterprise applications where AI agents practice tasks in simulated software. The deal deepens Mercor's model-training stack, pairing its network of 5M domain experts and APEX benchmarks with the environments those tasks run inside, and lands as Mercor reportedly seeks funding at a roughly $20B valuation.

title: A Taxonomy of Self-evolving Agents
blurb: This post presents a taxonomy of self-evolving agents, systems that improve their own capabilities over time rather than staying fixed after training. It organizes emerging approaches into categories, citing examples like Nous Research's Hermes Agent, which auto-generates reusable skills, and RSI Lab's recursive attempts to discover new algorithms.

title: EdgeRefine: Privacy-Utility Balance for Graphs via Jaccard Sampling under Edge Differential Privacy
blurb: EdgeRefine is a local differential privacy framework for graph neural networks that improves the privacy-utility trade-off through adaptive edge refinement, estimating edge-existence probabilities with Jaccard similarity to guide noisy edge removal. Under a privacy budget of 2.5, it improves node classification accuracy over prior methods by 17.8% on ACM and 19.7% on Cora, while holding graph classification degradation near 5%.

title: Sources: Manus investors and management are discussing unwinding Meta's $2B buyout at the same valuation, with Tencent in talks to become the largest investor (Zijing Wu/Financial Times)
blurb: Manus is in talks to unwind Meta's $2B buyout at the same valuation, with Tencent lined up to become its largest investor, according to the Financial Times. The reversal would pull a marquee AI startup out of a US tech giant's orbit and back toward Chinese capital, a rare undoing of a completed deal. For founders, it signals how quickly geopolitics and investor pressure can rewrite ownership even after a nine-figure exit looks locked.

title: ChatGPT is now a partner for your most ambitious work
blurb: OpenAI released ChatGPT Work, an agent that takes action across your apps and files and stays with a project for hours by breaking it into steps and finishing them independently. It ships with Codex built in and runs on GPT‑5.6, OpenAI's new frontier model, which pushes it to state of the art at multi-step reasoning and producing docs, sheets, and slides that follow your templates. Scheduled Tasks let it keep work moving while you are away, turning Slack and Teams messages into updated docs on its own.

title: Fidji Simo says she has decided to leave her full-time role at OpenAI and transition to being a part-time adviser after her medical condition worsened (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Fidji Simo is stepping down from her full-time role at OpenAI to become a part-time adviser after her medical condition worsened. Simo joined last year to run OpenAI's applications business, the consumer and product side that sits apart from the research org. Her exit pulls a senior operator out of the seat responsible for turning OpenAI's models into revenue-generating products, at a moment when the labs are pushing hardest to move down the stack and compete with their own customers.

title: Mercor is in talks for a $20B valuation
blurb: Mercor is in talks to raise at a $20B valuation, double the $10B it commanded in its $350M Series C last October. The jump is backed by real traction: founder-CEO Brendan Foody says annualized revenue run rate crossed $2B, up 100% in four months, and the company just acquired agent-training startup Deeptune, absorbing its whole team. The AI data-labeling and training layer is compounding fast enough to price a company at double its value in three quarters, even after a rough early 2026 of a data breach and contractor lawsuits.

title: A look at Meta Superintelligence Labs' growth in the past year, including a top-tier RL environment and compute ramp that could catch up to Anthropic and OpenAI (SemiAnalysis)
blurb: Meta Superintelligence Labs has closed much of the gap on Anthropic and OpenAI in the past year, building a top-tier reinforcement learning environment and ramping compute toward frontier scale. The RL setup, where models learn from feedback on their own outputs, is now considered among the best in the industry, and the compute buildout gives Meta the raw capacity to train at parity. The talent-and-spending push that looked like flailing a year ago is starting to resemble a credible third contender.

title: Paris-based AI voice startup Gradium raises $100M seed, backed by Nvidia
blurb: Gradium, the Paris voice AI startup spun out of Kyutai, reopened its seed round to reach $100M total, with Nvidia joining backers like Eric Schmidt and Xavier Niel. The company is spending the money to open a Bay Area office and fight for talent near Anthropic, Google, Meta, and OpenAI, a notable admission that even Europe's strongest AI hub still pulls toward the US. Gradium builds ultra-low-latency audio models and has already landed customers like Renault, but it faces ElevenLabs, valued at $11B, and Google's Gemini.

title: UK’s Lendable Raises Debt Backed by Personal-Loan Portfolio
blurb: Lendable is raising £500M ($671M) by selling securities backed by its personal-loan portfolio, funding a deeper push into consumer lending. Asset-backed deals like this let digital lenders scale loan books without diluting equity or waiting on deposits, turning existing loans into cheaper capital for new ones. The move signals that UK fintechs are maturing into balance-sheet operators, tapping securitization markets that were once the preserve of incumbent banks.

title: How Gamma Hit $100M ARR With a Team of 50: CEO Grant Lee’s Top 4 Lessons.  And Top 5 Mistakes
blurb: Gamma reached $100M ARR with 50 employees, 50 million users, and 600,000 paying subscribers, doing most of it with zero sales and marketing spend. Growth came from word of mouth, which meant the first 30 seconds of the product had to be magical enough that users told friends unprompted. CEO Grant Lee's one regret is waiting too long to add sales, and his other lessons hold up: become a creator before you run creator marketing, dogfood to build conviction rather than just catch bugs, and revisit pricing constantly because set-it-and-forget-it packaging is dead.

title: OpenAI’s No. 2 Executive to Step Down in Latest Leadership Shake-Up
blurb: Fidji Simo, OpenAI's applications CEO and the company's second-ranking executive, is stepping down and will not return from medical leave. Her exit leaves a top operating role open right as OpenAI moves toward an IPO and reorganizes around commercializing its products. The pattern for founders watching the labs is that senior leadership at the frontier stays unstable even as the businesses mature, and the people who build the revenue engine are exactly the ones hardest to replace on short notice.

title: OpenAI and Google are providing advanced AI models to Singapore-based subsidiaries of Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent, exposing a gap in US AI controls (Madhumita Murgia/Financial Times)
blurb: OpenAI and Google are supplying advanced AI models to the Singapore-based subsidiaries of Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent, routing frontier capability to Chinese tech giants that US export rules were meant to wall off. The gap sits in geography, not intent, because the controls track where a company is incorporated rather than who ultimately owns it. For founders building on US frontier APIs, it is a reminder that the compliance perimeter around this technology is porous and likely to tighten, which could reshape who your vendors are allowed to serve.

title: Bezos opens Blue Origin to outside investors for the first time in 26 years
blurb: Bezos is opening Blue Origin to outside investors for the first time since founding it in 2000, raising about $10B at a $130B pre-money valuation, with Coatue in for roughly $4B and Bezos himself adding around $2B. The end of 26 years of self-funding signals that staying in the space race now costs more than even one of the world's richest people can carry alone. The pressure is SpaceX, freshly public near a $2tn valuation, and the capital only matters if Blue Origin gets New Glenn back to the launch pad after its failed static-fire test.

title: Anthropic’s $1.2T secondary valuation jumps 550% in a year, overtaking OpenAI
blurb: Anthropic's shares are trading on secondary markets at an implied $1.2 trillion valuation, up 550% in a year and ahead of OpenAI's roughly $908 billion. The move is less about fundamentals than market structure: employees and early backers refuse to sell, so near-total scarcity is pushing prices up independent of any new revenue or product. That gap matters because these are illiquid minority positions running mostly through SPVs the company has disavowed, which means anyone pricing Anthropic ahead of its confidential IPO is reading a number the company neither controls nor can police.

title: NVIDIA backs the French researchers who built voice AI’s foundations at $100M, 7 months after launch
blurb: Gradium, the Paris startup spun out of research lab Kyutai, extended its seed round to $100M with NVIDIA joining, seven months after leaving stealth. Its four founders wrote EnCodec, SoundStream, and Moshi, the papers that underpin most modern voice AI, including the systems OpenAI ships. The tension is the story: the researchers who built the field's plumbing are now trying to commercialize it against the companies that already profited from their work, in a voice AI market projected to grow from $7.7B in 2026 to $20.7B by 2031.

title: Fidji Simo steps down from OpenAI’s no. 2 role
blurb: Fidji Simo, OpenAI's No. 2 and CEO of Applications, is stepping down from her full-time role after a medical leave ran longer than expected, moving to a part-time advisory seat. Her exit removes the executive who consolidated OpenAI's business and product operations and was widely seen as a successor-in-waiting, leaving Altman to fill that vacuum right as the company eyes an IPO. It also lands at a weak spot for the consumer business she ran, where ChatGPT growth cooled and missed internal revenue targets, pushing OpenAI to lean harder into coding tools it still trails Anthropic on.

title: Enterprises using multiple AI models are underestimating failure rates by 2.25x
blurb: Enterprises stacking multiple AI models to cover each other's blind spots are underestimating their real failure rates by 2.25x, according to a study evaluating 67 frontier models from 21 providers. The limit on orchestration isn't how often models disagree, it's the co-failure ceiling: the share of prompts where every model in the pool is wrong at once, which no router or cascade can fix. Teams are paying in latency, infrastructure, and governance risk to chase performance gains that the math says don't exist, and the same math gives a cost-free test for when orchestration actually pays off.

title: Exclusive: Fanvue doubles ARR to $200M, targets $2B in a market it pegs at $1.3T
blurb: Fanvue doubled its ARR to $200M in the past year and is now targeting $2B in a creator economy it pegs at $1.3T. The platform leans on AI creators and tools to grow while keeping its team small, betting that AI-native monetization scales faster than the human-only model it competes against. The gap between its $200M today and the $2B goal is the tell: the company is wagering that the addressable market is large enough that execution, not demand, is the only real constraint left.

title: OpenAI debuts ChatGPT Work, an agentic tool for automating business workflows
blurb: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, a new agentic mode powered by GPT-5.6 and Codex that takes actions across your connected apps and files rather than just answering prompts. It plugs into Slack, Teams, Gmail, Google Drive, Salesforce, and SharePoint, decomposes a goal into subtasks, and works over hours, asking for human approval before sensitive actions. It ships to Pro, Enterprise, and Edu subscribers first, with the Codex experience folded into a single desktop app available to every plan, including free.

title: Blue Origin Seeks $10B at $130B Valuation, Reporting Shows
blurb: Blue Origin is reportedly raising $10B at a $130B valuation, its first outside round after years as a Bezos-funded project, with $2B from Bezos, $4B from Coatue, and $4B from other institutions. The raise puts a firm number on a company that still sits an order of magnitude below SpaceX's ~$2T market cap, and it comes months after New Glenn exploded on the pad, with a return to flight targeted before year-end. The bet investors are buying is that Blue's pivot into satcom, lunar contracts, and space systems eventually rivals Amazon's $2.6T.

title: MiniMax Joins China AI Fundraising Rush With $2 Billion Deal
blurb: MiniMax is raising as much as $2 billion through new shares and convertible bonds, joining a wave of Chinese tech companies tapping markets to fund the AI buildout. The rush signals that China's frontier labs are now competing on capital access, not just model quality, and are willing to take on debt to keep pace with the compute spending of US rivals. For founders selling into or against Chinese AI, the takeaway is that the well-funded field just got deeper, and the buildout is being financed at national scale.

title: Market data platform startup Databento closes $97M round after drawing $300M in investor demand
blurb: Databento raised $97M led by New Enterprise Associates after fielding more than $300M in investor demand, over three times what the round took. The startup sits its own servers inside exchange data centers to capture futures, options, and equities data at the source, selling it on demand through one API instead of the months-long procurement that Bloomberg and LSEG terminals require. Revenue rose 6.65 times year-over-year, enterprise retention has held above 97%, and the company reached profitability with 24 employees before raising a dollar of this round.

title: Open-source AI developer tool Ollama raises $65M to grow its platform
blurb: Ollama raised $65M in a Series B led by Theory Ventures, bringing total funding to $88M and pushing the open-model tooling layer further up the stack. The tool now reaches 8.9 million developers a month and is used inside 85% of the Fortune 500, including regulated buyers in government, healthcare, and finance. The interesting move is the neocloud pivot: the same command and OpenAI-compatible API that runs a local 7B model swaps to a 400B cloud model with one string, so Ollama monetizes inference without breaking the local workflow developers already trust.

title: How to Scale a Neobank from 0 to 60,000 Customers
blurb: A founder's breakdown of taking a neobank from zero to 60,000 customers, covering the acquisition, retention, and compliance moves that actually moved the number. The through-line is that early growth came from a narrow, underserved segment rather than a broad launch, and the hard part was building trust and operational infrastructure fast enough to keep the customers you win. The thread is worth reading if you're building anything regulated, where distribution is only half the problem and staying compliant while you scale is the other half.

title: Paris-based Aria raises €7 million and launches €240 million debt facility to tackle Europe’s late-payment crisis
blurb: Aria, a Paris-based fintech, raised a €7M Series A extension led by 115K and opened a €240M debt facility to buy suppliers' invoices and pay them upfront, financed through a Nomura-led securitisation fund. The pitch is a real market: late payments cost European small businesses an estimated €100B in trapped cash flow each year and push 38 UK companies under every day. Embedded invoice financing that lives inside marketplaces, ERPs, and vertical SaaS is becoming the wedge, turning a back-office pain point into infrastructure other software companies rent rather than build.

title: An AI agent startup just let its agent run its $100M fundraise
blurb: Lyzr used its own AI agent to run its $100M Series B, fielding 130+ investors without a founder leaving the desk.

title: Oxylabs becomes Lithuania’s second most valuable private company at $3.6B
blurb: Oxylabs raised $130M from Warburg Pincus at a $3.6B valuation, its first outside money in the decade since it was founded and enough to make the Vilnius proxy business Lithuania's second most valuable private company behind Vinted. The unglamorous web data infrastructure company got there by turning away investors for ten years while building to a $350M run rate serving more than 350,000 customers. The $3.6B mark values it at roughly 10.3x revenue, a richer multiple than rival Bright Data reportedly commands, and makes it the second Tesonet-incubated company to hit unicorn status after Nord Security.

title: Carlyle agrees to sell US data center power and infrastructure platform Copia to EQT; sources: the deal values Copia at $2.6B, a fivefold return for Carlyle (Financial Times)
blurb: Carlyle agreed to sell Copia, its US data center power and infrastructure platform, to EQT in a deal that values Copia at $2.6B and hands Carlyle a fivefold return. The exit shows how the money is flowing to the picks-and-shovels layer of the AI buildout, where power and physical infrastructure now command private-equity multiples that used to belong to software. For founders, the signal is that whoever controls the electricity and land under the models is capturing a growing slice of the value.

title: Tencent in Talks to Become Manus’ Largest Holder, FT Reports
blurb: Tencent is in talks to become the largest shareholder in Manus, the Chinese agentic AI startup whose acquisition by Meta was blocked by Beijing, according to the Financial Times. The deal would fold one of China's leading agent builders under a domestic tech giant after regulators killed its path to a US owner. For founders, it signals that frontier AI startups are increasingly being pulled into national spheres, with cross-border exits now a bet Beijing and Washington can veto.

title: MiniMax is looking to raise as much as $1.9B by selling 35.6M shares at ~$34 each and offering ~$830M in convertible bonds due 2027 (Bloomberg)
blurb: MiniMax is looking to raise as much as $1.9B, selling 35.6M shares at roughly $34 each alongside about $830M in convertible bonds due 2027. The raise signals that Chinese AI labs are still pulling in frontier-scale capital even as Western investors question whether the model layer can hold its value. For founders building on top of these labs, the takeaway is that the field's biggest players are stocking up on compute and runway, and the pressure to differentiate above the model keeps climbing.

title: Gates Heir’s Shopping App Claimed Sales It Didn’t Drive
blurb: Phia, the shopping-assistant startup co-founded by Phoebe Gates, took credit for sales it didn't actually drive, according to Bloomberg. The app pitches itself as a personal shopping assistant that hunts down the lowest prices, but its affiliate tracking appears to have claimed commissions on purchases customers would have made anyway. For founders building on affiliate or attribution models, it is a reminder that last-click credit is easy to inflate and hard to defend once a partner audits where the sales really came from.

title: Drone Startup Helsing Alters Staff Stock Plan Before Round
blurb: Helsing, the European defense startup, overhauled its employee share program right before closing the largest funding round in its history, a change that has alienated some staff. The move shows how much leverage the hottest defense-tech companies now hold over their own people, rewriting equity terms while demand for capital lets them dictate terms. For founders, it is a reminder that the same market heat that fills your cap table can also tempt you into changes that cost you the trust of the team that built the company.

title: SK Hynix Makes Biggest Foreign Debut in US
blurb: SK Hynix raised $26.5 billion in its US share sale, the largest first-time offering ever by a foreign company on an American exchange. The South Korean memory chipmaker now has a deep new fundraising channel aimed squarely at investors hunting for ways to play the AI infrastructure trade. The money follows the compute: as demand for high-bandwidth memory tracks the AI buildout, the suppliers one layer below the frontier labs are raising at a scale that used to be reserved for the labs themselves.

title: MiniMax Shares Drop on $2 Billion Fundraising Plan
blurb: MiniMax shares dropped after the Chinese AI company disclosed plans to raise $2 billion through a share placement and convertible-bond offering. The market read the dilution and the capital intensity as a warning that even well-backed AI labs need to keep tapping investors to fund the compute race. For founders, it is a reminder that frontier AI economics still favor raising ahead of need, and that the public markets punish that math even when the growth story stays intact.

title: Web data scraping infrastructure startup Oxylabs reels in $130M in its first funding round
blurb: Oxylabs, a Lithuanian web-scraping company bootstrapped since 2015, raised $130M from Warburg Pincus at a $3.6B valuation in its first-ever outside round. The company already runs more than $350M in ARR across 350,000 customers, routing billions of daily requests through a pool of over 175 million consumer device IPs to collect public web data at scale. The demand is coming from the agentic web, where AI agents now browse more than humans do and need a live feed rather than a stale index, which is where scraping infrastructure becomes the pick-and-shovel layer for training data and autonomous agents alike.

title: Prime Intellect raises $130M at $1B valuation for its AI training platform
blurb: Prime Intellect raised $130M at a $1B valuation, with Nvidia's NVentures, Intel Capital, and Dell Technologies Capital in the round. The company rents GPU clusters tuned for the increasingly common workflow where teams don't train models from scratch but customize open-source ones in simulated environments, using its Verifiers and Prime-RL toolkits to spin up sandboxes and parallelize runs across thousands of chips. The bet is that reinforcement-learning fine-tuning becomes a standard layer of the stack, and the infrastructure to run it is worth backing before the demand fully lands.

title: AI agent startup Lyzr reportedly raising $100M at $500M valuation
blurb: Lyzr, a two-year-old startup that helps enterprises build AI agents, is reportedly raising about $100M at a $500M valuation, double its March mark, with $400M of investor interest already circling. Its pitch is speed: a no-code Agent Studio that turns a natural-language prompt into a deployed agent in minutes, packaged with RAG, guardrails, multi-agent orchestration, and 100-plus templates the company says cut deployment work by more than 70%. The round tracks where capital is flowing now, toward the tooling layer that makes agents fast to ship rather than the models underneath.

title: Kraken Technology Group makes waves as Europe’s newest maritime defence unicorn with €152.9 million Series B
blurb: Kraken Technology Group raised €152.9M ($175M) at a valuation near €874.1M ($1B), making the London-based maker of uncrewed surface vessels Europe's newest maritime defence unicorn. The round drew NATO Innovation Fund, the British Business Bank, Rheinmetall, and the UK's National Security Strategic Investment Fund, with several early backers converting their earlier investments to equity. Defence is now pulling institutional and state-linked capital the way SaaS once did, and dual-use hardware startups building for NATO are commanding software-scale valuations off contract wins rather than ARR.

title: Dear SaaStr: Our VC Is Encouraging Us to Hire Aggressively and Increase the Burn Rate. Should We Listen to Them?
blurb: When a big VC pushes you to hire aggressively and raise your burn rate, the advice is wrong or self-interested about two-thirds of the time, especially from the largest funds. A fund that wants to own more of you worries less about your zero-cash date than you should. Most first-time founders actually under-spend early and figure out how to turn $X of hiring into $Y of return on their own by $6M to $10M ARR. The useful version points at specific accretive hires, a VP of marketing or an expanding sales segment, and lets you decide.

title: Analysis: Architect and the $11 Trillion AI Capital Markets
blurb: The next AI frontier may be financial infrastructure rather than better models, as GPUs, compute capacity, and inference become tradable financial assets in their own right. An estimated $11T will flow into AI infrastructure between 2024 and 2029, roughly $7T of it financed by debt, which creates real demand for hedging, derivatives, and price discovery. Architect's ComputeConnect, a regulated exchange linking GPU derivatives to physical compute delivery, is an early sign that crypto-native market structure is moving into the largest technology investment cycle in history.

title: New Lithuanian unicorn: Oxylabs ends bootstrapped streak after securing €113.6 million at €3.1 billion valuation
blurb: Oxylabs raised €113.6 million from Warburg Pincus at a €3.1 billion valuation, its first outside money since 2015 and the second bootstrapped unicorn to come out of Lithuania's Tesonet venture builder. The Vilnius proxy-and-web-data company is repositioning from premium scraping infrastructure into the layer that feeds real-time web data to AI agents and models. As agents start browsing the open web far more than people do, the companies that own compliant, live data pipes are becoming the picks-and-shovels play, and investors are now paying unicorn prices for that access.

title: Echodyne opens $40M radar factory near Seattle to meet booming demand for drone detection
blurb: Echodyne opened a $40M radar factory near Seattle that will eventually build more than 2,500 drone-detection radars a month, roughly 30,000 a year. Demand is being driven by battlefield drone proliferation in Ukraine and the fast-growing low-altitude economy of commercial drone operations that need airspace monitoring. The company's radar already feeds systems from Anduril, Axon, and Northrop Grumman, and it was just named primary radar provider on a $490M U.S. Air Force contract. The CEO's framing captures where counter-drone is heading: the only way to defend against mass is with mass.

title: Wayve completes first-ever private securities market transaction
blurb: Wayve completed the first-ever transaction on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, letting employees cash out $85M in vested equity while the company stays private. The sale is the first real test of PISCES, the UK framework that lets scaleups run intermittent liquidity auctions on public-market infrastructure. It matters because late-stage British tech has long crossed the Atlantic for liquidity, and a working domestic route gives founders a reason to keep their cap tables and their talent at home.

title: Supply chain startup Auger, led by ex-Amazon operations chief, raises $50M and lands big customers
blurb: Auger, the supply chain startup founded by former Amazon operations chief Dave Clark, raised $50M in Series B led by Eclipse, bringing total funding to $150M with about 130 employees and customers including Meta, Fanatics, and Kimberly-Clark. Clark's bet is that general-purpose AI can generate insights but can't run a specialized domain like supply chain, so Auger sits on top of existing ERP and warehouse systems and uses AI agents plus optimization models to make and execute routine decisions autonomously. At Fanatics, roughly 85% of the decisions Auger manages already happen without a human.

title: Invest Europe: Venture capital reaches second-highest level on record
blurb: European private capital held above €260 billion for the second year running in 2025, and venture capital delivered its second-highest transaction value on record at €35.3 billion despite fewer deals. The money is concentrating: larger financings above €30 million are taking a growing share, buyouts still command 72% of the market, and ICT plus healthcare pulled record investment while consumer goods fell to a decade low. For founders, the signal is that capital is resilient but flowing to bigger checks in innovation-led sectors, not spreading evenly across the field.

title: Europe’s newest defence unicorn raises $175m Series B
blurb: Kraken Technology Group raised $175M in Series B funding led by DTCP, hitting a $1B valuation and becoming Europe's newest defence unicorn. The London company builds uncrewed autonomous maritime vessels for surveillance, logistics, and coastal combat, with contracts already signed with the UK Ministry of Defence, NATO partners, and US Special Operations Command. As defence climbs to the top of Europe's agenda, capital that first chased drones and AI is now moving into maritime tech, where investors like the NATO Innovation Fund and Rheinmetall are betting on protecting the continent's subsea infrastructure.

title: The $28M bankruptcy buy that became a $3.5B nuclear IPO and the CIA officer who made it possible
blurb: Standard Nuclear bought TRISO fuel maker X-energy's spun-out assets out of bankruptcy for $28M and rode it to a $3.5B IPO, with a former CIA officer brokering the deal. TRISO fuel, the pebble-sized uranium kernels that most next-gen reactor designs depend on, has almost no domestic supply, so owning the manufacturing base matters more than owning any single reactor. The lesson for founders is that the durable position in a hyped market is often the unglamorous chokepoint everyone else assumes someone will build.

title: AI writing startup co-founded by DeepMind creative lead raises $13M seed investment
blurb: Marker, a London startup from ex-DeepMind creative lead Jon Steinback, raised $13M in seed funding led by Index Ventures, with angels including Slack's Cal Henderson and Hugging Face's Thomas Wolf. It bills itself as a reimagined word processor that writes with the writer, not for them, covering ideation, drafting, revision, and collaboration. The pitch is a bet against AI slop: as LLM-generated documents flood inboxes, Marker is wagering that writers will pay for tools that protect the craft rather than automate it away.

title: Kraken – no, not that one – becomes UK’s latest unicorn
blurb: Kraken Technology Group, a British maritime defence company building uncrewed surface vessels, raised $175M at a $1B valuation in a Series B led by DTCP, with backing from the British Business Bank, the NATO Innovation Fund, and Rheinmetall. Defence is now minting unicorns on the strength of contracts and deployments, not projected TAM. Kraken already holds deals with the UK MoD, NATO partners, and USSOCOM, has its platforms deployed in active conflicts, and runs localized manufacturing with Rheinmetall, Anduril, and Davie Shipbuilding.

title: 20VC x SaaStr: Washington Lifts the Fable 5 Ban, Sam Floats Handing the Government 5%, and Why Every Tech Giant Is Racing to Become the Next IBM
blurb: Washington lifted the 19-day Fable 5 ban, but the pre-approval precedent it sets is what should worry builders.

title: AI puts UK startup funding back in the big leagues with strongest first half since 2022, new data shows
blurb: UK startups raised €14.8B ($17B) in the first half of 2026, their strongest opening half since 2022 and 102% up on a year earlier, with the country now taking 39% of all European venture capital. The money is not spreading out. Late-stage companies pulled 68% of the total, up from 42%, and four AI-heavy rounds alone (Isomorphic Labs, Nscale, Wayve, Ineffable Intelligence) accounted for €5.5B while deal count fell to 490. Capital is back at scale, but it is concentrating in a handful of large AI, infrastructure, and science bets rather than lifting the whole market.

title: Judge denies FTC request to presume Zillow-Redfin deal ‘unlawful’
blurb: A federal judge denied the FTC's motion to declare Zillow's $100M exclusive multifamily rental listings deal with Redfin "presumptively unlawful" before trial, citing "genuine disputes of material fact." The ruling keeps the burden on regulators heading into the August 24 bench trial, rather than forcing Zillow and Redfin to prove the deal legal from a losing position. The core fight is market definition: the FTC wants rental listings treated as a concentrated national market, while the companies argue competition is local and includes Google and social platforms chasing the same ad dollars.

title: Nandan Nilekani leaves GP role at Fundamentum as it launches $200M third fund
blurb: Nandan Nilekani, co-founder of Infosys and architect of India's Aadhaar identity system, is stepping down as a general partner at Fundamentum, the firm he co-founded in 2017, as it launches a third fund targeting about $200M. He stays on as anchor investor and mentor, with the fund now led by co-founder Sanjeev Aggarwal and three longtime partners. Fund III will back eight to 10 early-stage consumer tech, fintech, and AI startups with initial checks near $10.5M each, a generational handoff at one of India's best-known venture firms.

title: How Europe’s B2B M&A hit $129B in its best quarter ever
blurb: Europe's B2B M&A hit a record $129B in Q2 2026, its strongest quarter ever for dealmaking in the sector. Private equity drove most of the volume, hunting for cash-generating software and services businesses while public market exits stayed shut. The signal for founders is that the exit path is bending toward strategic and PE buyers rather than IPOs, which favors durable margins and recurring revenue over growth-at-any-cost stories.

title: Oxylabs, which develops web data scraping infrastructure, received a $130M investment at a $3.6B valuation from Warburg Pincus, its first outside investment (Mike Wheatley/SiliconANGLE)
blurb: Oxylabs, a bootstrapped web-scraping infrastructure company, took its first outside money: a $130M investment from Warburg Pincus at a $3.6B valuation. The number is a marker for how much AI has reshaped the data-collection layer, where scraping pipelines that feed model training and real-time retrieval have quietly become critical infrastructure. A company that stayed private and self-funded for years is now worth more than most venture-backed AI startups, which says the picks-and-shovels of the data supply chain are where a lot of the durable value sits.

title: From Arrival’s collapse to the UK’s biggest fleets: How 3 engineers built a better delivery vehicle in a London warehouse
blurb: Three engineers from the failed EV startup Arrival built Minimal, an electric cargo vehicle now running in some of the UK's biggest delivery fleets including Royal Mail. Rather than chase Arrival's sprawling ambition, they narrowed the problem to one purpose-built vehicle designed in a London warehouse. The lesson is that a focused product from a small team can outlast a well-funded company that tried to do everything at once.

title: AI coding tool hole illustrates a big problem with human in the loop
blurb: Wiz disclosed GhostApproval, a vulnerability pattern in six AI coding assistants (Amazon Q Developer, Claude Code, Augment, Cursor, Google Antigravity, and Windsurf) where a malicious repository uses symlinks to escape the workspace sandbox while the approval prompt hides the dangerous target from the developer. The agent's own reasoning often flags the sensitive file, but the CWE-451 UI misrepresentation means users approve what looks like a harmless local edit, potentially yielding remote code execution.

title: Building Accessibility Into a Canvas-based Product
blurb: Figma's canvas takes over rendering from the browser to hit performance targets like infinite zoom and real-time multiplayer, but that also means the browser's built-in accessibility tree stays empty because there's no semantic DOM to build from. The team's fix is a Mirror DOM: invisible synthetic elements that shadow the scenegraph, cache accessibility state per layer, and make surgical updates so screen readers and keyboard navigation work against a file that's really just one canvas input.

title: You shouldn't trust Trusted Publishing
blurb: The author argues that debates over whether Trusted Publishing is worth trusting rest on a category error, since the scheme secures machine-to-machine authentication between CI/CD and package indexes, not human judgment about a maintainer. Built on OIDC federation and now adopted by npm, RubyGems, crates.io, and NuGet, it swaps long-lived, over-scoped tokens for short-lived, minimally scoped credentials, which the author calls a net win despite its complex data model and per-provider handling.

title: TTHE: Test-Time Harness Evolution
blurb: This paper introduces Test-Time Harness Evolution (TTHE), which optimizes an LLM agent's harness during evaluation using only unlabeled execution traces, maintaining a population of candidate harnesses that an agentic proposer refines and a judge commits based on execution-derived proxy signals. Across text-to-SQL, competitive programming, software engineering, and agentic tool-use tasks, TTHE improves fixed ReAct-style baselines without updating model weights or requiring gold labels, though it flags proxy reliability as the core open challenge.

title: Injective SDK on npm infected with cryptocurrency wallet stealer
blurb: Attackers compromised a legitimate contributor's GitHub account for the Injective Labs SDK and published a malicious version 1.20.21 of the @injectivelabs/sdk-ts npm package that steals wallet private keys and seed phrases when key-generation functions are called. The package pulls 50,000 weekly downloads and was fetched 310 times before deprecation, with stolen secrets base64-encoded and exfiltrated to an Injective public endpoint to blend in with legitimate traffic.

title: Show HN: Getting GLM 5.2 running on my slow computer
blurb: Colibrì runs GLM-5.2, a 744B-parameter MoE model, on a consumer machine with about 25GB of RAM by keeping the ~17B dense params resident at int4 and streaming the 21,504 routed experts from disk with a per-layer LRU cache. The engine is a single ~2,400-line C file with no BLAS, Python, or GPU required, and uses GLM-5.2's own int8 MTP head for lossless speculative decoding at 39-59% draft acceptance.

title: npm 12 Disables Install Scripts by Default to Reduce Supply Chain Risk
blurb: npm 12 ships with install scripts disabled by default, so lifecycle hooks (preinstall, install, postinstall) and implicit node-gyp builds no longer run unless developers opt in via npm approve-scripts and commit an allowlist to package.json. The release also deprecates 2FA-bypass granular access tokens, stripping their ability to publish directly and forcing automated publishing toward trusted publishing (OIDC) or staged publishing with human approval.

title: Speedier type checks in TypeScript 7.0 as first stable Go release ships
blurb: Microsoft shipped the first stable release of TypeScript 7.0, the compiler rewritten from JavaScript to Go, delivering roughly 10x faster type checking and project load times on large codebases. The native port keeps the existing type system and syntax intact, so the speedup lands without requiring changes to existing TypeScript projects.

title: TLS certificates for internal services done right
blurb: The author lays out how to serve TLS on internal-only services using split-horizon DNS so a VPN client and a public resolver return different IPs for the same domain, letting a public CA like Let's Encrypt issue real certificates. The trade-off is trading self-signed certs installed on every client for a single WAF at the reverse proxy that rejects non-VPN traffic, demonstrated with NetBird, acme.sh, and nginx in front of Grafana.

title: DeepSWE: Measuring Frontier Coding Agents on Original, Long-Horizon Engineering Tasks
