blurb: DeepSWE is a benchmark of 113 original, long-horizon software engineering tasks written from scratch across 91 open-source repositories and five languages, never contributed upstream so reference solutions stay out of training data, and graded by hand-written verifiers that accept any correct implementation. An independent LLM judge disagrees with those verifiers 1.4% of the time versus 32.4% for SWE-Bench Pro's inherited tests, and the tasks touch 5.5x more code while spreading frontier agents across a wider score band.

title: Safely run AI-generated code in Cloud Run sandboxes
blurb: Google Cloud Run sandboxes are in public preview, giving developers a native runtime that spawns millisecond-latency isolated execution boundaries inside existing Cloud Run instances for running AI-generated code, untrusted binaries, and agent workloads. Enabling it takes a single deployment flag, and a benchmark spun up 1,000 sandboxes that start, execute, and stop with an average of 500ms latency, replacing hand-built container clusters or third-party microVM runtimes.

title: How GitHub gave every repository a durable owner
blurb: GitHub validated durable ownership for every active repository across its 14,000-repo internal org in under 45 days, archiving roughly 8,000 unused repos and making ownership mandatory at creation time. The post details how a service-to-repo mapping in their Service Catalog left team, docs, and one-off repos unowned, which blocked secret-scanning remediation because rotating a secret without a known owner risked disrupting unknown consumers.

title: Profiling in PyTorch (Part 3): Attention is all you profile
blurb: Part 3 of Hugging Face's PyTorch profiling series walks through attention by building it up from primitives and reading how each variant looks under the profiler, starting with a naive causal implementation of matmul, scaling, masking, softmax, and a second matmul. The post then profiles progressively faster versions on an A100, from in-place ops to `scaled_dot_product_attention` and custom kernels, showing how each trick collapses the trace differently rather than covering every optimization in detail.

title: 3100 Opinions on Code Review in an AI World: Building Causal Theory from Practitioner Discourse
blurb: The authors mined 38,709 engineering blogs and Reddit threads, coding a 3,100-document sample with an LLM-assisted pipeline into a causal model of 26 constructs and 67 relationships about how coding agents change code review. Their central claim is that review is the control point deciding an agent's effect on a codebase, and that the team, not the AI, sets whether that effect is positive.

title: JetBrains seeks to unify fragmented AI-based software development with governance suite
blurb: JetBrains released JetBrains AI for Teams and Organizations, a governance suite that adds a single control layer over most coding assistants, CLIs, and IDEs, including Claude, Codex, Gemini, Junie, and the JetBrains IDEs, with VS Code support coming. It bundles cloud agents for long-running tasks, JetBrains Context for shared project understanding across tools, and JetBrains Central for access control, cost management, and usage tracking.

title: Building a real-time AI tutor for 5-year-olds
blurb: Ello's team details the architecture behind an AI tutor that teaches math and reading to kids ages 4-9 with sub-second responses on every turn. They scrapped the standard tool loop, whose 2-3 second first-token latency and 30 tokens-per-second decode left 3-4 seconds of dead air per turn, long enough for a child's attention to wander and, in playtests, to train kids to tune the tutor out.

title: Building the AI Retrieval Infrastructure Behind 20 Billion+ Vectors at HubSpot
blurb: HubSpot's engineering team details VaaS (Vector as a Service), a centralized platform that fronts a self-managed Qdrant cluster holding 20B+ vectors and serves semantic search to 38+ internal teams. The post covers the architecture choices behind the scale: synchronous reads versus asynchronous writes, HNSW's low-latency-for-high-memory trade-off, and cost tuning through quantization and on-disk payload storage.

title: Tool-Making and Self-Evolving LLM Agents in Low-Latency Systems
blurb: This paper replaces the per-request code-generation loop in production LLM agents with a tool-making pipeline that compiles repeated SOP steps into validated, versioned tools before deployment, grounding synthesis in live execution traces and backend schemas. Deployed on a Fulfillment Center alarm-triage agent over a 44-node SOP, tool calls cut p50 latency 42% and end-to-end error rate up to 53% by suppressing run-to-run variance in repeated steps.

title: Show HN: FableCut – A browser video editor AI agents can drive (zero deps)
blurb: FableCut is a Premiere-style non-linear video editor that runs entirely in the browser and exposes its whole timeline as a single project.json describing media, clips, tracks, effects, and keyframes. Any process that can write JSON can cut video, so an agent speaking MCP or REST edits the timeline while the open UI hot-reloads via server-sent events in about 150ms, with zero npm dependencies.

title: Stop being the code review bottleneck
blurb: PostHog argues that as agents write code faster than any human can review, the fix is to review less code yourself rather than review faster, delegating first-pass reviews to a pipeline of agents. The post details engineer Paul D'Ambra's qa-swarm setup, where four reviewer agents with distinct instructions and models flag issues that a triage step sorts into actionable, nits, or ambiguous, escalating only the last to a human.

title: Better Auth Joins Vercel
blurb: Better Auth, the open source framework-agnostic auth library that took over Auth.js and NextAuth.js, is joining Vercel to keep building auth in the open with more resources behind it. The team plans to focus on auth for AI agents, where apps need to grant secure, revocable access to act on a user's behalf.

title: Put a Deterministic Gate Between Generated Code and Main
blurb: This tutorial builds a deterministic pre-merge gate as a small POSIX shell script that inspects a diff before generated code reaches main, rejecting patches that exceed a file or line budget, touch sensitive paths like `.env`, `infra/`, or `migrations/` without escalation, or fail Git's structural checks. It deliberately owns only size, path ownership, and structural validity, leaving correctness to tests, type checks, and human review so the pass/fail result stays explainable.

title: On the Correctness of Software Merge
blurb: This paper defines formal correctness criteria for three-way merge tools, requiring results to be both parsable (syntactically valid per the language grammar) and universal (incorporating all and only the edits from each branch, applying shared edits once), with universality formalized via category-theoretic pushouts. Across 43,774 file merge scenarios from 76 open-source Java projects, the authors' structural tool reported no incorrect merges while existing tools including Git's companion merge produced several.

title: PERFOPT-Bench: Evaluating Coding Agents on Software Performance Optimization
blurb: PERFOPT-Bench introduces a benchmark for evaluating coding agents on the full performance-engineering loop: profiling executions, diagnosing cross-layer bottlenecks, editing without breaking correctness, and verifying reproducible speedups. Testing 7 agent stacks on 7 long-horizon tasks, the authors find optimization is workload-dependent rather than model-determined, and that raw speedup is unsafe to score because some large gains come from benchmark-specific shortcut exploitation.

title: Launch HN: Context.dev (YC S26) – API to get structured data from any website
blurb: Context.dev launched a single API that scrapes, crawls, and extracts structured data from any website via a JSON schema, alongside brand data, company classification, and image pulls. It replaces the internal crawlers, cutoff-frozen agent knowledge, and multi-vendor stacks teams stitch together, with Mintlify using it to turn a GitHub repo URL into a branded docs site in under 10 minutes of integration.

title: Interview with Mitchell Hashimoto about Ghostty and Zig
blurb: Mitchell Hashimoto, creator of Terraform, Vault, and the rest of the HashiCorp stack, discussed why he built Ghostty, a fast, feature-rich, natively cross-platform terminal emulator, in Zig. He started it to relearn GPU and desktop systems programming after years on distributed systems, and argues against turning terminals into general application platforms since the monospaced-grid model is good at something the browser and desktop are not.

title: A road to Lisp: Why Lisp
blurb: The author argues that learning Lisp makes you a better programmer because its macro system lets you extend the language itself, growing it toward your problem rather than bending your problem to fit the language. Framing the difficulty through Paul Graham's Blub paradox, the piece walks through macros, the REPL, and conditions and restarts to show the capabilities that lower-power languages leave you unable to even perceive.

title: I built a production-ready MCP server template so you don't have to — here's the architecture
blurb: The author shares a production-ready MCP server template built around a server factory that creates a fresh instance per request, letting the same code back both stdio (editor clients) and stateless Streamable HTTP behind Express. The six-part architecture covers Zod validation at boot and per tool call, an SSRF-hardened fetch_url with hostname allowlisting and byte caps, and structured logging to stderr so stray stdout writes never corrupt the JSON-RPC stream.

title: The Memory Wall of Green Software: Empirical Energy Evaluation of Memento Design Pattern
blurb: This paper measures the energy cost of the Memento design pattern's state-snapshot strategies using Intel RAPL hardware telemetry, comparing classic full snapshots against differential delta-encoding across state sizes from 10 MB to 200 MB. Differential encoding cuts energy up to 65.8% at mid-scale but hits a memory wall at 200 MB, where GC thrashing and non-linear power spikes erase the algorithmic gains entirely.

title: GitLake: Git-for-data for the agentic lakehouse
blurb: GitLake proposes a Git-for-data layer that lifts single-table Iceberg snapshots into lakehouse-wide commits, branches, and merges, so agents can work on isolated branches while humans review and publish. Pipelines run on temporary branches and land through a final merge, making all outputs visible atomically or not at all. The paper also reports production lessons and correctness findings from an Alloy model of the core branch-and-merge abstractions.

title: ImputeViz: A Visual Analytics Dashboard for Diagnosing Missing Data and Comparing Imputation Methods
blurb: ImputeViz is a visual analytics dashboard for diagnosing missing data and comparing imputation methods, bringing MICE, Random Forest, XGBoost, and kNN into one interactive environment with heatmaps, co-missingness summaries, and MCAR/MAR/MNAR diagnostics. It reports MAE, RMSE, Delta RMSE, and runtime per method on a shared target and mask, and adds gKNN, a geographically informed kNN variant that exposes which regions drive each estimate.

title: Synthetic Data Generation for Financial AI Research with NVIDIA NeMo
blurb: NVIDIA published a walkthrough of an iterative pipeline that generated 502,536 unique financial news headlines across 13 topic categories, using NeMo Data Designer for synthesis and NeMo Curator for semantic deduplication. A naive 50,000-headline run lost 65% to near-duplicates, so the workflow loops generation, global dedup, and farthest-from-centroid few-shot selection to force diversity. The full corpus took 82 iterations and roughly 6 days on a single 8-way B200 node.

title: Building Accessibility Into a Canvas-based Product
blurb: Figma detailed how it rebuilt accessibility for its canvas, which bypasses the browser's DOM and the accessibility tree that screen readers and keyboard navigation depend on. Their solution is a Mirror DOM that shadows the scenegraph with synthetic, invisible elements, letting assistive tech navigate layers, hear edits announced, and operate the editor without the free semantics an HTML app inherits.

title: Figma's Loredana Crisan talks taste, last-minute decisions and the art of prioritisation
blurb: Figma chief design officer Loredana Crisan, who joined last September after nearly a decade leading design at Meta, described how the company finalizes its Config conference product lineup only the weekend before to keep the event unrehearsed. She framed prioritization as "an art that we pretend is a science" and cast Figma's push against encroaching AI tools as a bid to keep human creativity central.

title: Designing For Distressed Users: Why Mental Health Apps Shouldn’t Follow Every UI Fashion
blurb: Mental health apps hemorrhage users, with median 30-day retention at 3.3% and even category leaders losing half their users within ten days. The author argues that chasing UI trends optimized for attention and novelty directly conflicts with what distressed users need, since an unconventional icon or flashy animation becomes cognitive load exactly when users have the least to spare.

title: When Brand Guidelines Ruin Your Website (And How to Fix It)
blurb: Brand guidelines built for print or advertising often break on the web, where rigid color palettes fail contrast checks, fixed type scales ignore responsive breakpoints, and locked spacing rules fight fluid layouts. The author argues designers should treat guidelines as a starting point and adapt them to digital constraints, documenting web-specific tokens for accessibility, states, and reflow rather than forcing brand rules to override usability.

title: Koto’s new type foundry is redefining the rules of font design
blurb: Koto, the global creative studio behind custom typefaces like Polkadot and Stack Overflow's brand font, launched CcType, a foundry selling fonts on a one-time purchase model rather than restrictive licensing. Its debut release, CcTimeline, is a display sans serif with adjustable axes and OpenType controls built to shift across a wide emotional range, from functional product UI to expressive campaign work.

title: AI coding tool hole illustrates a big problem with human in the loop
blurb: Wiz disclosed GhostApproval (CWE-451), a symlink-based pattern affecting six AI coding assistants including Amazon Q Developer, Claude Code, Cursor, Google Antigravity, Augment, and Windsurf, where a malicious repository escapes the workspace sandbox and can achieve RCE by hiding the true write target from the human approval prompt. AWS, Cursor, Google, and Anthropic have patched, while Augment and Windsurf acknowledged the report but have not confirmed fixes.

title: You shouldn't trust Trusted Publishing
blurb: Trusted Publishing isn't a scheme humans are meant to trust, the author argues; it's machine-to-machine authentication built on OIDC federation, so evaluating it as something you personally can or can't trust is a category error. Adopted across PyPI, npm, RubyGems, crates.io, and NuGet, it shrinks the number of long-lived, over-scoped credentials maintainers hold by issuing short-lived, minimally scoped publishing tokens tied to CI/CD identity. It still isn't foolproof, since a credential lives somewhere in the flow.

title: Microsoft is rewriting Windows patch guidance because of AI
blurb: Microsoft is advising organizations to shorten Windows update deployment timelines, warning that AI is cutting the time attackers need to weaponize vulnerabilities after a patch ships. The recommended settings are aggressive: quality update deferrals under three days, deadlines of zero to one day, and grace periods of no more than two. Defenders can use the new Windows Autopatch report in Intune to surface still-exposed devices, enable Hotpatch for reboot-free fixes, and enforce update compliance through Conditional Access.

title: Injective SDK on npm infected with cryptocurrency wallet stealer
blurb: Attackers compromised a contributor's GitHub account for the @injectivelabs/sdk-ts npm package, publishing malicious version 1.20.21 that harvests wallet private keys and mnemonic seed phrases when developers call key-generation functions, exfiltrating base64-encoded secrets to an Injective Labs endpoint to blend in. The package sees 50,000 weekly downloads and the malicious build was fetched 310 times before the maintainer reverted to clean release 1.20.23; audit dependency versions and rotate any keys handled by affected systems.

title: New GigaWiper Windows Backdoor Bundles Disk Wiping, Fake Ransomware, and Spyware
blurb: Microsoft dissected GigaWiper, a Go-based Windows backdoor that bundles three destructive modules: a raw disk wiper, a FlockWiper-based Windows-drive overwriter, and fake ransomware (Crucio-derived, .candy extension) that encrypts without saving the key. It also spies via multi-monitor screenshots and hidden VNC; Binary Defense tracks the same four hashes as BLUERABBIT, a likely Iran-nexus tool targeting Israeli organizations, so prioritize early detection and clean offline backups.

title: Attackers Exploit 'Ill Bloom' Vulnerability to Drain $3.1 Million From Cryptocurrency Wallets
blurb: Coinspect disclosed a cryptocurrency wallet flaw it calls Ill Bloom, in which affected software generated recovery phrases with weak randomness, shrinking the seed-phrase space enough for attackers to derive keys and drain funds. A coordinated May 27 sweep drained roughly $3.1M from 431 wallets, with confirmed losses now past $5M; hardware and mainstream software wallets are unaffected, but users of older or lesser-known apps should check illbloom.org and rotate funds.

title: An off switch for dual use knowledge in AI models
blurb: Anthropic and AE Studio introduced GRAM (Gradient-Routed Auxiliary Modules), a method that isolates dual-use knowledge (such as advanced virology or offensive cyber techniques) into removable modules, letting one trained model serve both restricted and vetted-access versions instead of training separate filtered models. Unlike refusal training and output classifiers, which leave the underlying knowledge intact and jailbreakable, GRAM controls what the model knows. Results are preliminary and it has not been applied to any production Anthropic model.

title: Why we cannot wait for better post-quantum signature algorithms
blurb: Cloudflare is moving forward with ML-DSA for its post-quantum signature migration rather than waiting for better schemes, targeting full PQ security by 2029. ML-DSA carries real costs, being much larger on the wire and incompatible with optimizations RSA and ECC allowed, but successors like FN-DSA and NIST's nine on-ramp candidates will not be ready in time. Deploy the algorithm you have now; harvest-now-decrypt-later already threatens authentication, not just encryption.

title: New Helix vishing group emerges in SharePoint data theft attacks
blurb: A new data-extortion group tracked as Helix is breaching Microsoft 365 tenants through vishing and device-code phishing, impersonating managers via caller-ID spoofing before registering a rogue MFA authenticator for persistence and bulk-exfiltrating SharePoint data. ReliaQuest ties Helix to defunct BlackFile and ShinyHunters infrastructure and flags its clearest fingerprint: automated `contentclass:STS_Site` and wildcard searches run from 179.43.185[.]230 using the python-requests/2.28.1 user-agent.

title: Dormant GitHub Accounts Help Attackers Blend In While Mapping Corporate Orgs
blurb: Datadog Security Labs disclosed several overlapping campaigns using 50+ dormant GitHub "ghost" accounts, created two to five years ago and left inactive, alongside compromised OAuth tokens and PATs to enumerate corporate organizations, repositories, and members via the GitHub API. Because much of the API is reachable unauthenticated, the low-and-slow reconnaissance blends into normal traffic, and in confirmed cases attackers cloned a private repository.

title: npm 12 Disables Install Scripts by Default to Reduce Supply Chain Risk
blurb: npm 12 ships with install scripts disabled by default, so dependency lifecycle hooks (preinstall, install, postinstall) and implicit node-gyp builds no longer run unless explicitly allowlisted via npm approve-scripts. Git and remote-URL dependencies are also opt-in now, closing the most common paths for supply-chain compromise. GitHub is also deprecating 2FA-bypass GATs: they lose sensitive account actions in early August and direct publishing in January 2027, pushing automated releases toward trusted publishing (OIDC) or staged human-approved publishes.

title: New Forg365 phishing platform uses AI to target Microsoft 365 accounts
blurb: Forg365, a new phishing-as-a-service platform, targets Microsoft 365 accounts by combining AiTM and device-code phishing with AI-generated lures built directly into its operator dashboard. It ships a ForgCookie browser extension for Chrome, Edge, and Brave that auto-refreshes Microsoft SSO cookies, letting operators retain access to compromised accounts without re-authenticating.

title: 12 Million Impacted by Data Breach at Japanese Telco KDDI
blurb: KDDI confirmed that a June 17 breach exposed the email addresses of 12.2 million people and the passwords of 7.6 million, after attackers exploited a zero-day in software underpinning email infrastructure shared by five ISPs including JCOM, NIFTY, and BIGLOBE. The vendor is still developing a patch for the flaw, which had been exploited since May, and KDDI is forcing a mandatory password reset across all affected accounts.

title: GitHub AI agent leaks private repositories via prompt injection attack
blurb: Researchers at Noma Security disclosed GitLost, a prompt-injection attack against GitHub's preview Agentic Workflows in which a crafted public GitHub issue coerces the AI agent into reading private repos and posting their contents in a public comment. The attack needs no credentials, malware, or software flaw, and a minor wording change bypassed GitHub's prompt-based guardrails, so treat agent read access to private repos as an exfiltration path.

title: Beware What You Autocomplete: Forensic Attribution of Backdoored Code Completions
blurb: This paper introduces CodeTracer, a forensic framework that traces backdoored LLM code completions back to the specific malicious fine-tuning samples that caused them, using only the fine-tuning corpus and the reported miscompletion event. It extracts a behavioral fingerprint from the unsafe output, narrows to semantically relevant training samples, and uses LLM-based reasoning for attribution. Across three vulnerability cases, ten backdoor attacks, and sixteen baselines, the authors report high attribution accuracy, low false-identification rates, and robustness against adaptive attacks.

title: GodDamn Ransomware Uses PoisonX Driver to Disable Endpoint Defenses
blurb: GodDamn ransomware, assessed as a rebrand of Beast and Monster and tracked to developer Hyadina, uses the PoisonX kernel driver ("g11.sys") in a BYOVD attack to disable AV and EDR before encrypting. PoisonX is a malicious driver that its developers got validly signed by Microsoft, letting Windows auto-load it. Symantec observed a June attack chaining AnyDesk remote access and NirSoft-based credential harvesting; defenders should enforce Microsoft's vulnerable driver blocklist.

title: Hidden backdoor in Tenda routers goes unpatched as company ignores warnings from cybersecurity researchers — Chinese company's firmware allows admin access without a password
blurb: CERT/CC disclosed CVE-2026-11405 on July 6, an undocumented authentication backdoor in Tenda router firmware that grants full admin access to the web management interface without valid credentials by falling through to a hidden password check. No patch exists and Tenda has not responded, so defenders running the affected FH1201, W15E, AC10, AC5, and AC6 builds should pull these devices off the internet and block WAN-side access to the admin interface.

title: TLS certificates for internal services done right
blurb: The author argues that split-horizon DNS is the cleaner way to secure internal-only services, avoiding the operational drag of self-signed certificates. A single domain (e.g. grafana.tuxnet.dev) resolves to a public IP for outside resolvers and an internal IP over the VPN, letting a public CA like Let's Encrypt issue valid certs while a WAF rejects non-VPN traffic. The tradeoff: one WAF rule on the reverse proxy replaces distributing a private CA to every client or telling users to ignore TLS errors.

title: ‘HalluSquatting’ Turns AI Hallucinations Into Botnet Delivery Mechanism
blurb: Researchers from Tel Aviv University, Technion, and Intuit detailed HalluSquatting, an untargeted promptware technique that pre-registers the fake repository and package names that LLMs commonly hallucinate, then plants malicious instructions inside them. Hallucination rates hit 85% for repo-cloning prompts and 100% for skill installations across tools like Cursor, Copilot, and Gemini CLI, letting attackers build agentic botnets that bypass firewalls and execute code on any device via the assistant's built-in terminal.

title: Skills Registry Threat Models
blurb: Skills registries for AI agents are a strict superset of package-manager clients: installing one skill can trigger install commands across npm, pip, cargo, brew, and more from a manifest the user never read, while the skill body joins the agent's system prompt as a prompt-injection vector and inherits the session's existing bash, file, and network grants. This analysis walks the loader, registry, and runtime, showing that the worst exposures are deliberate design decisions that pass every static scanner rather than flaggable bad lines.

title: Extortion crew hijacks Microsoft 365 accounts via fake passkey setup
blurb: The Pink extortion crew is hijacking Microsoft 365 accounts through vishing calls where an attacker poses as IT and walks employees through a fake Entra passkey enrollment on a spoofed login page. The passkey and BIP-39 "recovery key" prompts are sleight of hand to keep victims occupied while the crew uses the harvested credentials and MFA factors to enroll their own passkey on the real account.

title: From Legacy Documentation to OSCAL: An MCP-Based Agent Pipeline for Threat-Informed Continuous Compliance in Critical Infrastructure
blurb: This paper introduces an MCP-grounded multi-agent pipeline that turns natural-language system descriptions of OT environments into audit-ready NIST OSCAL artifacts, decoupling LLM reasoning from deterministic retrieval against threat-intel sources to curb hallucinated CVEs and attack paths. On a synthetic water-utility scenario it hit 0.90 CVE recall and perfect D3FEND recall, but errors shifted upstream: a single misextracted asset yields real-but-irrelevant CVEs, making manual review the remaining control point.

title: Darktrace finds AI gateway with Amazon Bedrock access hijacked for cryptomining
blurb: Darktrace detailed a cloud intrusion in which an internet-exposed LiteLLM-Proxy EC2 instance, carrying an instance profile with Amazon Bedrock access, was hijacked on June 12 to run the XMRig Monero miner. The instance had SSH open to the internet on port 22 with brute-force activity observed, and the miner beaconed to a pool over HTTPS on port 443; scope AI gateway credentials tightly and remove public SSH exposure.

title: 15-Year-Old Linux Vulnerability ‘GhostLock’ Earns Researchers $92k From Google
blurb: Nebula Security disclosed GhostLock (CVE-2026-43499), a 15-year-old use-after-free in the Linux kernel task-cleanup path that affects every major distribution since kernel 2.6.39 and was patched in April. The researchers published exploit code demonstrating local privilege escalation to root and a container escape in Google's kernelCTF, earning a $92,337 bounty, so unpatched hosts and multi-tenant container fleets should prioritize the April kernel update.

title: TRACE: A Two-Channel Robust Attribution Watermark via Complementary Embeddings for LLM-Agent Trajectories
blurb: TRACE is a watermarking scheme that lets developers prove attribution of LLM-agent trajectories even when a reseller has full read/write access to the trajectory logs. It embeds two complementary channels: a distortion-free selection channel keyed on local content that resynchronizes after deletions, and a tally channel keyed on the log's structure that survives arbitrary rewriting. On ToolBench and ALFWorld it matched unwatermarked success rates while staying detectable under 70% step deletion, making log corruption the only way to strip attribution.

title: Most data brokers won’t tell you what happened to your deletion request
blurb: A UC Irvine study sent deletion and opt-out requests to every reachable broker on California's public registry in fall 2025, roughly 322 deletion and 360 opt-out requests, and found that about 70% never reported the outcome of a deletion despite the 45-day CCPA deadline. Around 22% demanded identity verification on opt-outs, which the CCPA prohibits, and overall response rates sat between 26% and 38%, meaning the privacy rights consumers use most see the least compliance.

title: Threshold Authorization Without Threshold Signatures: Signature-Agnostic MPC Custody
blurb: This paper introduces a dual-gate custody architecture that decouples member authentication from threshold authorization, so digital-asset quorums no longer depend on threshold signature schemes that break under the post-quantum transition. Each member signs approvals with any EUF-CMA scheme while the quorum produces an information-theoretic threshold seal from Shamir shares, making a move from ECDSA to SLH-DSA or ML-DSA a key rotation rather than a protocol redesign. An adversary holding t or more signing keys but no coefficient shares still cannot forge the seal.

title: Accenture Confirms Data Breach After Hacker Claims Source Code Theft
blurb: Accenture confirmed a breach after a threat actor on the PwnForums hacker forum claimed theft of 35GB of internal data, including Azure access keys, RSA and SSH keys, configuration files, and source code, with a private accenture.com Azure DevOps repo screenshot offered as proof. The company said it remediated the source and reported no operational impact, but the exfiltration method, initial access vector, and any exposure of personal data remain unclear.

title: Mini-Programs, Mega-Problems: Unveiling OAuth-based Authentication Misuses in Mini-Programs via Dynamic Analysis
blurb: Researchers introduced MINIAUTH, a dynamic-analysis framework that pinpoints and executes OAuth login flows in super-app mini-programs to find runtime authentication misuses that static tools miss. Scanning 44,273 WeChat and 2,721 Baidu mini-programs, it surfaced 1,834 misuse cases, including client-side identity forgery via exposed credentials, auth bypass through static or plaintext identifiers, and a cryptographic flaw in Baidu's OBA APIs that permits brute-forcing session keys. Findings drew CNVD/CNNVD IDs, underscoring the need for platform-level OAuth safeguards over reliance on third-party developers.

title: Prismata: Confining Cross-Site Prompt Injection in Web Agents
blurb: Prismata introduces contextual least privilege for autonomous web agents, dynamically deriving per-content trust labels that constrain what an agent can see and do to contain cross-site prompt injection. Its confinement guarantees bound any mislabeling so permissions can only decrease in privilege, and it redacts untrusted content and restricts capabilities without developer annotations. Across recent published web-agent attacks, including adaptive variants, the authors report substantially reduced attack success while preserving benign task utility.

title: EU 'Chat Control' snoopfest returns after vote to kill it falls short
blurb: The EU's controversial 'Chat Control' regulation, which would mandate client-side scanning of encrypted messages, revived after MEPs failed to secure enough votes to block it. The proposal now heads back into negotiation, keeping mandatory content-scanning of private communications on the table for encrypted messaging platforms operating in the bloc.

title: An unnamed US county – perhaps in Ohio – paid $1M extortion demand to cybercriminals
blurb: An unnamed US county, reportedly in Ohio, paid roughly $1M to extortionists after a ransomware-style compromise of its systems. The disclosure adds to the pattern of local governments quietly settling extortion demands rather than absorbing prolonged outages, so defenders in the public sector should validate offline backups and segment county networks to blunt the leverage attackers gain from encrypting or exfiltrating records.

title: GigaWiper Combines Multiple Malware for System-Level Sabotage
blurb: Microsoft reported GigaWiper, a Go-based modular backdoor active since October 2025 that folds a physical-disk wiper, destructive encryption using unrecoverable random keys, and full C&C (RabbitMQ, Redis, MinIO) into a single implant capable of BSOD triggers, log clearing, and Windows-installation wipes. Attributed to the Crucio ransomware developer with code shared from FlockWiper, it signals a shift toward destruction-plus-extortion, so treat affected hosts as unrecoverable and prioritize offline backups.

title: AI Gateways Offer Attackers the Keys to the Kingdom
blurb: Darktrace investigated an incident where a threat actor compromised an externally exposed EC2 instance hosting an AI gateway connected to Amazon Bedrock and used it for cryptomining. Because gateways centralize access to foundation models, cloud infrastructure, proprietary data, and IAM, the same initial-access path could be reused for credential theft, model manipulation, or deeper cloud persistence. Treat AI gateways as high-value aggregation targets: restrict external exposure, scope their IAM permissions tightly, and monitor them for anomalous Bedrock and identity activity.

title: Token-Flow Firewall: Semantic Runtime Auditing for Persistent AI Agents
blurb: This paper introduces TokenWall, a runtime firewall that audits the natural-language token flows between a persistent AI agent's memory, tool arguments, and retrieved files, intercepting risky semantic flows before they reach privileged sinks. On CIK-Bench it cut attack success rate to 12.5% while keeping a 97.4% benign pass rate and adding 0.69 seconds of latency, positioning full-coverage pre-execution mediation as a practical alternative to sparse auditing or remote large-model oversight.

title: EU Parliament greenlights Chat Control 1.0
blurb: The European Parliament passed the interim "Chat Control 1.0" regulation, extending permission for suspicionless mass scanning of private communications until 2028, despite 314 of 607 voting MEPs opposing it. The rejection motion failed to reach the required absolute majority of 361 votes, and a symbolic carve-out for encrypted communications changes little since providers do not scan those messages anyway.

title: Secure QR Codes: Authenticity Verification via EdDSA Signatures and CBOR Certificates
blurb: This paper proposes a scheme to sign QR codes against spoofing (quishing) attacks on public infrastructure like parking machines, using EdDSA (Ed25519) signatures with CBOR-encoded certificates and ZLIB compression to fit cryptographic integrity within a QR code's static capacity. Because offline-only models can't revoke keys at smart-city scale, the authors add a hybrid Web PKI mode using JWKS endpoints, a central trust registry, and URL fragments for real-time validation while staying readable by standard cameras.

title: Efficient Safety Alignment of Language Models via Latent Personality Traits
blurb: This paper introduces Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), a safety-tuning method that adversarially stabilizes personality-anchored representations using just 66 harm-agnostic psychometric statements instead of large harmful-prompt datasets. The authors report near-zero attack success on HarmBench across direct requests and five jailbreak methods with no benchmark degradation, and the training completes in minutes on a single GPU using 75x fewer examples than standard Latent Adversarial Training.

title: Multi-Agent Firewall Architecture for Privacy Protection of Sensitive Data in Interactions with Language Models
blurb: This paper introduces an open-source, user-facing firewall that intercepts both web and programmatic LLM traffic across HTTP(S) and WebSocket to prevent sensitive-data and proprietary-code leakage. It pairs a browser extension and proxy with a multi-agent pipeline combining deterministic detectors and LLM-driven semantic analysis, reaching F1 scores up to 94.93% in optimal configurations.

title: Detecting Ladder Logic Bombs in IEC 61131-3 PLC Programs using ESBMC-PLC+: A Formal Verification Approach with Trigger Synthesis
blurb: This paper introduces ESBMC-LLB, a formal-verification tool that detects Ladder Logic Bombs hidden inside PLC function-block bodies that existing ladder-diagram verifiers drop from their IR. On the Iacobelli 2024 dataset it caught all 30 bombs and recovered every trigger, and hit 149/150 on PLC-Defuser's SWaT corpus, though detection fell to 49% against nonlinear non-termination bombs as the SMT solver timed out.

title: Ethereum Foundation says AI agents find real bugs, but most are false positives
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation's Protocol Security team reported that coordinated AI agents surface real vulnerabilities in its systems software, cryptographic code, and smart contracts, but most candidates are wrong, duplicate, or out of scope. The agents found and fixed a remotely-triggerable panic in libp2p's gossipsub, yet the team says the work shifted from finding bugs to triaging them, since a candidate only counts once researchers independently reproduce the failure against real code.

title: Ransomware Negotiator Gets 70 Months in Prison for Aiding BlackCat Attacks
blurb: A federal court sentenced Angelo Martino, a former ransomware negotiator, to 70 months in prison for feeding BlackCat operators his clients' confidential negotiating positions and insurance limits to inflate ransom demands across five victims. Two co-conspirators from DigitalMint and Sygnia, Ryan Goldberg and Kevin Martin, each received four-year sentences in May for deploying BlackCat, and law enforcement has seized $10M tied to the scheme.

title: Microsoft Reins in RoguePlanet Zero-Day Threat
blurb: Microsoft issued an out-of-band patch for RoguePlanet (CVE-2026-50656, CVSS 7.8), an elevation-of-privilege flaw in Windows Defender that lets a standard user escalate to SYSTEM. The fix ships in Malware Protection Engine 1.1.26060.3008; Microsoft rates exploitation "more likely" given low attack complexity and a public PoC, so treat this as a post-compromise privilege-escalation risk and prioritize patching endpoints running Defender.

title: Out of Sight: Compression-Aware Content Protection against Agentic Crawlers
blurb: This paper introduces CAPE, a framework that protects textual content from LLM agent crawlers by injecting human-invisible perturbations that survive rendering but corrupt the context-compression step agents use to fit their token budgets. Targeting compression rather than access controls or visible injection, CAPE raised agent information loss by up to 75.8% over the strongest baseline and transferred to LangGraph workflows and GitHub Copilot while leaving content visually identical to the original.

title: Microsoft Windows Installer Folder Delete Privilege Escalation
blurb: CVE-2025-27727 is a privilege-escalation flaw in the Windows Installer service (msiexec.exe, running as SYSTEM), where the MSI COM interface deletes arbitrary user-accessible folders because it blindly trusts paths stored in the TempPackages registry key without verifying the installer created them. A low-privileged user can chain the folder-deletion primitive into SYSTEM code execution via planted rollback scripts in a recreated C:\Config.Msi; Microsoft patched it in April 2025.

title: Microsoft expects more Windows security updates from AI-discovered flaws
blurb: Microsoft said Windows customers should expect more security updates per Patch Tuesday as its MDASH agentic scanning harness, which validates candidate flaws across multiple AI models before a Windows-specific false-positive pipeline, accelerates vulnerability discovery in critical binaries. Human engineers still review and validate all proposed fixes before release, and Microsoft is updating its Secure Development Lifecycle to account for AI-enabled attack techniques.

title: Scot NHS Trust probes email stuffup involving maternity patients' data
blurb: NHS Greater Glasgow and Clyde launched an investigation after a misdirected email exposed maternity patients' personal data to unintended recipients. The trust has referred the incident to the UK Information Commissioner's Office, which can levy GDPR penalties for healthcare data breaches, and is notifying affected patients as it reviews how the disclosure occurred.

title: Accenture admits to 'isolated matter' after crook tries to flog alleged 35GB haul
blurb: Accenture confirmed a security incident it characterized as an "isolated matter" after a criminal advertised an alleged 35GB data haul stolen from the consultancy for sale. The company disputes the scope and says no client systems were affected, but defenders should treat any exposed Accenture-managed credentials or access tokens as potentially compromised pending confirmation.

title: Patch for Windows Defender 0-day could allow attackers to fill hard disk
blurb: Microsoft's patch for the RoguePlanet zero-day (CVE-2026-50656), a Defender flaw that granted remote attackers admin control of Windows 10 and 11 even with real-time protection off, introduced a new defect that lets attackers exhaust all disk space. Researcher NightmareEclipse reports the defense-in-depth mitigations cause mpengine.dll to cache Zone.Identifier ADS files without size limits, bypassing Defender's normal quarantine caps so an attacker can write unbounded data to disk.

title: Microsoft closes book on Nightmare Eclipse's RoguePlanet zero-day
blurb: Microsoft patched the RoguePlanet zero-day exploited by the threat group Nightmare Eclipse, closing an actively abused flaw that had been under exploitation before the fix shipped. Defenders running affected Microsoft products should apply the update immediately and hunt for prior compromise, since exploitation predated the patch and any exposed systems should be treated as potentially breached until logs are reviewed.

title: Shared API keys expose AI agents at 69% of enterprises, new VentureBeat research finds
blurb: Sharing one API key across a fleet of AI agents means a single compromised agent inherits every permission the key touches, and the audit trail dies at the credential level because one account can't attribute actions to individual agents. VentureBeat's June 2026 Pulse Research found 69% of 107 enterprises run agents with credential sharing somewhere in their deployments, and 54% have already hit an agent security incident or near-miss. Scope each agent to its own identity and enforce per-agent authorization at runtime.

title: Network of 200 GitHub Repositories Used for Malware Infection
blurb: Socket disclosed Operation Muck and Load, a campaign of 222 lure GitHub repositories across 190 accounts that pose as a DNS/subdomain scanner built on the legitimate dnsub project while hiding a whitespace-obfuscated PowerShell command in a Go module. The chain fetches resolver material from dead drops on Pastebin, YouTube, Telegram, and Google Docs to deploy AsyncRAT, Quasar RAT, Vidar infostealer, and XMRig cryptominers.

title: UK Government Rolls Out Agentic AI Defense Plan Alongside Industry Pledge
blurb: The UK's NCSC launched Cyber Shield, a national-scale agentic cyber defense program that pairs frontier AI red and blue teams to discover, contain, and automatically remediate vulnerabilities across government and CNI boundaries. NCSC is soliciting partners from academia, critical infrastructure, and frontier labs to build six core capabilities, though critics note autonomous-AI defense does little against the credential and configuration failures that compromise most organizations today.

title: Palo Alto Networks Patches 13 Vulnerabilities
blurb: Palo Alto Networks patched 13 vulnerabilities across its products, the most severe being CVE-2026-0288, a high-severity set of PAN-OS buffer overflows that lets an unauthenticated attacker with network access to the firewall trigger DoS and potentially achieve code execution via crafted traffic. Exploitation risk drops sharply when access to the User-ID Terminal Server Agent is restricted to trusted internal IPs, and no in-the-wild attacks have been observed yet.

title: The open source library holding up your stack might have one maintainer
blurb: A new paper argues that "open source" hides fourteen distinct project types, and the differences in funding and governance predict how each holds up in production. The sharp case is critical digital infrastructure: components like OpenSSL, curl, and log4j sit under much of the ecosystem yet often rely on a handful of unpaid volunteers. For defenders, the typology is a way to triage dependencies by governance model rather than treating every import as equally maintained.

title: Former DigitalMint ransomware negotiator who duped clients sentenced to 70 months in jail
blurb: Former DigitalMint ransomware negotiator Angelo John Martino III was sentenced to 70 months for sharing clients' negotiating positions and insurance limits with BlackCat affiliates to extort $75.3 million from five U.S. companies he was hired to defend. The case exposes insider risk in incident-response supply chains: firms retaining third-party negotiators should compartmentalize victim data and audit who can access ransom ceilings and insurance details mid-engagement.

title: Barracuda strengthens identity security capabilities with Evo Security acquisition
blurb: Barracuda Networks acquired Evo Security, a Texas-based IAM and privileged access management specialist built for MSPs, and will fold its technology into the BarracudaONE platform. The integration adds access management, identity protection, and identity threat detection and response to BarracudaONE, letting partners manage authentication and access policies across multiple customer tenants from a single console.

title: Working with the enemy: Ransomware negotiator-turned cyber criminal jailed after working with hackers to extort clients
blurb: Angelo Martino, a ransomware negotiator at incident-response firm DigitalMint, was sentenced to 70 months in prison for secretly feeding BlackCat operators his clients' negotiating positions and insurance details to inflate ransoms. The DOJ seized over $10M in proceeds and charged two co-conspirators, including a Sygnia incident-response manager, underscoring insider risk within the IR and negotiation firms victims are supposed to trust.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability of LLM Jailbreaks via Internal Attribution Graphs
blurb: This paper introduces a mechanistic framework that diagnoses LLM jailbreaks by comparing internal computation graphs for clean and attacked prompts, revealing that adversarial prompts systematically suppress safety-relevant components, spawn attack-specific features, and reroute computation paths. The authors show that these structural deviations correlate strongly with unsafe outputs across multiple open-source models, and that targeted causal interventions on recurring vulnerability motifs measurably improve robustness, shifting jailbreak analysis from descriptive attribution to causal diagnosis.

title: KS-CFA: Control-Flow Attestation via Symbolic Replay Against Control-Flow Bending Attacks
blurb: KS-CFA is a new control-flow attestation scheme that detects control-flow bending attacks, where an adversary corrupts branch flags, loop counters, and other runtime data to steer execution along legal CFG edges. It records a control-flow trace and external inputs inside a RISC-V Keystone TEE, then lets the verifier replay the path via single-path symbolic execution to localize divergences, covering indirect calls, conditional and indirect jumps, and returns without enumerating measurement spaces. Prover-side overhead runs 6.7-32.2x over unattested execution.

title: CISA adds new exploited vulnerabilities to KEV catalog
blurb: CISA added three actively exploited vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on July 7, confirming in-the-wild attack activity against each. Federal civilian agencies face a binding remediation deadline under BOD 22-01, and private defenders should prioritize patching or mitigating the listed flaws given the confirmed exploitation.

title: AI Agents Are a New Kind of Identity & Most Organizations Aren't Ready
blurb: AI agents are a new class of non-human identity that governance models built for service accounts and API tokens fail to cover, argues a Dark Reading commentary drawing on Omdia analyst Todd Thiemann's work. Unlike static tokens, agents pursue goals autonomously, adapt, and touch repositories, pipelines, and deployments around the clock without a human in the loop or a clear audit trail. Treat them as identities to provision, monitor, and deprovision, but govern at machine speed and scale rather than reusing human-developer or service-account playbooks.

title: Open Models, Open Risks: Measuring Unsafe Generation in Text-to-Image Models In the Wild
blurb: This paper evaluated 200+ in-the-wild text-to-image models from Hugging Face against three jailbreak attacks, introducing an Advanced ASR metric because detector-only scores overstate real risk via semantic drift and generation artifacts. Most downstream models retained non-trivial safety without post-hoc safeguards, but the authors flagged a set of high-risk models, including seemingly benign releases whose unsafe behavior surfaced only under systematic testing, and reported them to Hugging Face.

title: Your next insider threat doesn’t have a badge. It has an API token
blurb: The author argues that the real AI-agent threat isn't prompt injection but authorized agents chaining individually-permitted actions into a harmful sequence, like an agent that reads a customer record, summarizes it, then emails it externally, where every step passes its own check but the combination is a breach. Because IAM and DLP evaluate one action at a time, they miss the outcome. Treat agents as insiders with API tokens instead of badges, and enforce authorization across the full sequence of tool calls, not per-step.

title: Functional and Secure Code Generation with Task Vectors
blurb: SecVecCoder applies task-vector arithmetic to LLM weights to generate code that is both functional and secure, avoiding the separate functionality-versus-security evaluation and post-generation patching of prior approaches. Across six coding LLMs on the CodeGuard+ benchmark, it raised trustworthy-completion rates by 2.1 to 36.0 percentage points, with gains up to 39.1 points on unseen CWE types, while adding under 0.6% decoding latency since only the weights change.

title: QIZ Security raises $17M seed round for post-quantum readiness platform
blurb: QIZ Security raised $17M in seed funding for a platform that inventories enterprise cryptographic assets over APIs and flags weak or outdated encryption ahead of the post-quantum migration. The raise lands weeks after a June 22 executive order pulled federal post-quantum deadlines forward to 2030-2031, sharpening the "harvest now, decrypt later" risk for organizations that can't yet map where their own cryptography runs.

title: Interpol cybercrime crackdown nets 5,800 arrests across 97 countries
blurb: Interpol arrested more than 5,800 alleged cybercriminals and seized $293M across 97 countries in Operation First Light, a three-month crackdown on social-engineering scams and money laundering. The operation identified over 142,000 victims, blocked more than 31,000 bank accounts tied to fraud, and dismantled schemes including business email compromise, romance scams, and impersonation rings.

title: QIZ Security Raises $17 Million for Cryptographic Governance Platform
blurb: QIZ Security raised $17M in seed funding, led by Bessemer Venture Partners and Merlin Ventures, for a platform that governs cryptographic posture and post-quantum readiness across on-premises, cloud, and hybrid environments. The system uses API-based integration to discover cryptographic assets, flag weak cipher suites and outdated protocols, and generate prioritized remediation plans ahead of Q-Day.

title: MLQENABLER: Enabling Secure Machine Learning Queries over Encrypted Database in Cloud Computing
blurb: This paper introduces MLQENABLER, a scheme that lets cloud providers run machine learning queries over client-encrypted databases without decrypting them, using an index-aid approach to preserve query capability that formal encryption would otherwise destroy. Initial experiments report an acceptable security level with only slight ML performance degradation, offering a path to outsource sensitive datasets to untrusted commercial clouds without exposing plaintext.

title: Reverse Engineering Compliance: A Dual-Graph Verification Framework for Auditing Legacy IT Security Concepts
blurb: This paper introduces ASSERT, a framework for verifying legacy IT security concepts before migrating them into machine-readable OSCAL artifacts under the NIS-2 Directive and BSI's Grundschutz++. It extracts legacy documents into formal graphs via ontology-based parsing, runs a five-class graph diff against an independently built reference state, and exports schema-valid OSCAL evidence. Testing on BSI's RecPlast dataset with local and commercial models surfaced a trade-off between discovering undocumented entities and enforcing schema conformance.

title: 3 ways AI powers service desk attacks and how to prevent them
blurb: IBM's 2025 Cost of a Data Breach Report found 16% of studied breaches involved attackers using AI, most often for phishing or deepfake impersonation, and the service desk is where that lands. AI sharpens three attack vectors: convincing impersonation via generated emails, voice, and video, faster reconnaissance that scrapes LinkedIn and company posts for personalization, and onboarding gaps where agents can't yet vouch for new hires. Require identity proofing before resetting MFA, issuing credentials, or approving sensitive changes, especially for first-day access requests.

title: Januscape: 16-Year-Old Linux KVM Bug Enables Cloud VM Escape Attacks
blurb: Researcher Hyunwoo Kim published details of Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a 16-year-old use-after-free bug in Linux KVM's shadow MMU that lets a guest VM corrupt host kernel memory and break guest-host isolation on multi-tenant x86 clouds like GCP and AWS. The public proof-of-concept reliably panics the host, taking down every co-tenant VM, and a full host code-execution exploit exists but remains unreleased.

title: Microsoft Patches RoguePlanet Defender Flaw That Can Grant SYSTEM Privileges
blurb: Microsoft patched RoguePlanet (CVE-2026-50656, CVSS 7.8), a race-condition privilege-escalation flaw in the Defender malware protection engine that could spawn a SYSTEM-level shell, nearly a month after the researcher Chaotic Eclipse disclosed it publicly. The fix ships automatically via engine version 1.1.26060.3008 with no admin action required, but the exploit worked on fully June-patched Windows regardless of whether real-time protection was enabled.

title: AI Coding Agents Found Triggering Endpoint Security Rules Built to Catch Attackers
blurb: Sophos analyzed a week of its own June 2026 endpoint telemetry and found AI coding agents including Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenAI Codex tripping behavioral detection rules built to catch human intruders, with credential access making up 56.2% of blocked activity. The agents were doing ordinary developer work, but actions like DPAPI browser-credential decryption and living-off-the-land payload fetches are indistinguishable from attacks, leaving security teams to tune endpoint policy for legitimate agent behavior.

title: Microsoft patches RoguePlanet Defender zero-day vulnerability
blurb: Microsoft patched a Microsoft Defender zero-day tracked as CVE-2026-50656, dubbed RoguePlanet, which let attackers win a race condition to spawn a SYSTEM-level command prompt on fully updated Windows 10 and 11 machines. The fix ships automatically through Malware Protection Engine version 1.1.26060.3008 rather than a manual patch, but it landed amid a public feud with the researcher who disclosed it and whom Microsoft has threatened with legal action.

title: The ‘Ghost' in the Database: Recovering Active ADFS Signing Keys via Machine DPAPI
blurb: Mandiant disclosed a Golden SAML variant in which manually rotated ADFS certificates leave active token-signing keys recoverable from Windows Machine DPAPI, letting attackers forge SAML assertions for any user and bypass MFA and conditional access. The technique hits enterprises that disable AutoCertificateRollover and rotate certificates by hand, and it sidesteps LSASS and the live ADFS process that most security monitoring watches, reducing the odds of detection.

title: Entra passkey enrollment vishing targets Microsoft 365 users
blurb: A threat actor tracked by Okta as O-UNC-066, tied to the Pink extortion operation, is calling Microsoft 365 users and convincing them to enroll an attacker-controlled Entra passkey, abusing the passkey registration campaign capability Microsoft opened to admins in May. The real-time PHP phishing kit relays credentials and adapts to each victim's MFA method, giving enterprises a fresh identity-attack surface to lock down as they roll out passkeys.

title: Microsoft is rewriting Windows patch guidance because of AI
blurb: Microsoft is advising organizations to shorten Windows update deployment timelines, warning that attackers now use AI to find and exploit vulnerabilities within days of a patch release. The company recommends deferral periods under three days, one-day deadlines, and Hotpatch for reboot-free installs, backed by a new Windows Autopatch report in Intune that flags devices still exposed after updates ship.

title: Dormant GitHub Accounts Help Attackers Blend In While Mapping Corporate Orgs
blurb: Datadog Security Labs flagged several overlapping campaigns using more than 50 dormant GitHub "ghost" accounts, created two to five years ago, plus dozens of compromised personal access tokens to systematically enumerate corporate GitHub organizations, repositories, and members through the API. Most queries hit public endpoints and blend into normal traffic, but attackers have already cloned at least one private repository, giving IT and security teams a reconnaissance vector that standard rate-limit and anomaly monitoring will miss.

title: Fake 7-Zip Installers Turn Devices Into Residential Proxy Nodes
blurb: Infoblox disclosed a China-based threat actor it calls Lurking Lizard, which has run an end-to-end malicious residential proxy business since 2022 using 230-plus lookalike domains and trojanized installers for 7-Zip, WhatsApp, and WireVPN to conscript compromised devices into proxy nodes. The operation impersonates proxy vendors like IPIDEA and SmartProxy and drop-catches expired domains to inherit their legitimacy, raising the enterprise stakes on software-supply-chain vetting and endpoint controls beyond official download sources.

title: New Helix vishing group emerges in SharePoint data theft attacks
blurb: A new data-extortion group called Helix is stealing SharePoint data through identity-based attacks, using vishing calls that impersonate managers, device-code phishing, and MFA abuse to gain and persist access before bulk-exfiltrating files. ReliaQuest researchers link Helix to the ShinyHunters and now-defunct BlackFile operations based on shared infrastructure and social-engineering tactics, flagging automated SharePoint enumeration from a fixed IP and python-requests user-agent as its strongest fingerprint.

title: Ubiquiti Patches Critical UniFi Flaws Across Connect, Talk, Access, Protect, and OS
blurb: Ubiquiti patched seven critical vulnerabilities across UniFi Connect, Talk, Access, Protect, and UniFi OS, including a maximum-severity CVSS 10.0 command-injection flaw in UniFi Connect and several 9.9-rated bugs enabling privilege escalation or arbitrary command execution by network-adjacent attackers. No exploitation of these flaws has been observed, but CISA recently confirmed three separate UniFi OS vulnerabilities were weaponized in the wild, raising the urgency for admins to apply the fixed versions.

title: New Forg365 phishing platform uses AI to target Microsoft 365 accounts
blurb: A new phishing-as-a-service platform called Forg365 targets Microsoft 365 accounts, combining adversary-in-the-middle and device-code attacks with AI-generated lures and a ForgCookie browser extension that auto-refreshes Microsoft SSO cookies to maintain access without re-authentication. Researchers at ZeroBEC note the integrated AI panel lowers the cost of both crafting custom lures and building the platform itself, signaling more MFA-bypassing threats aimed at enterprise Microsoft 365 tenants.

title: Hackers exploit Roundcube flaw to spy on academic researchers
blurb: A China-linked espionage cluster Proofpoint tracks as UNK_MassTraction has since May exploited Roundcube webmail flaws CVE-2024-42009 and CVE-2025-49113 at U.S. and Canadian universities, using a malicious email to deploy the IceCube stealer and harvest credentials, cookies, and 2FA data. The campaign targets physics, engineering, and national-security research staff, chaining to the SquareShell PHP webshell and VShell backdoor for remote code execution on internet-facing mail servers.

title: GitHub AI agent leaks private repositories via prompt injection attack
blurb: Noma Security researchers disclosed GitLost, a prompt-injection attack that tricks GitHub's preview Agentic Workflows into reading private repositories and posting their contents to a public issue, without stolen credentials or malware. The finding is a procurement warning for enterprises piloting agentic workflows tied to Claude or Copilot, since any agent granted org-wide read access can be turned into a data-exfiltration path by an unauthenticated attacker filing a crafted issue.

title: Streamline identity lifecycle management in Google Workspace with new inbound SCIM support
blurb: Google Workspace reached general availability for its inbound SCIM APIs, letting admins sync the Workspace directory in real time with any SCIM-compatible identity provider, HR system, or custom app instead of building custom integrations on the directory APIs. Provisioning, updates, and instant deprovisioning now propagate to downstream apps like Gemini Enterprise, closing the orphaned-account gap and easing compliance audits across Business and Enterprise tiers.

title: CISA Adds 4 Actively Exploited Adobe, Joomla, and Langflow Flaws to KEV
blurb: CISA added four actively exploited vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, including three CVSS 10.0 flaws in Adobe ColdFusion, Joomlack Page Builder, and JoomShaper SP Page Builder that enable unauthenticated remote code execution. The ColdFusion path-traversal bug was exploited within hours of disclosure, and the two Joomla builder flaws have already dropped web shells and rogue Super User accounts, making patching to the fixed builds a same-day priority for affected sites.

title: Writer AI Flaw Could Let Agent Previews Leak Session Tokens Across Tenants
blurb: Sand Security researchers disclosed WriteOut, a now-patched critical vulnerability in enterprise AI platform Writer that broke tenant isolation and let an attacker hijack any logged-in user's account with a single preview link. Because the malicious agent's sandbox harvested victim session tokens across separate organizations, an outsider could access private chats, documents, connectors, and LLM credentials, and seize admin control depending on the victim's role.

title: Flaws in some of the most popular AI coding tools left developers wide open to attack
blurb: Researchers at Wiz disclosed GhostApproval, a category-level flaw affecting six leading AI coding tools including Amazon Q Developer, Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, and Google Antigravity, where malicious repositories abuse symlinks to escape agent sandboxes and gain remote code execution on developer machines. Amazon, Cursor, and Google have shipped fixes and rated it critical or high, with no evidence of exploitation in the wild.

title: 'GodDamn' Ransomware Uses BYOVD to Smite US Companies
blurb: Symantec observed the Hyadina ransomware group deploying "GodDamn," a rebranded locker that loads a Microsoft-signed malicious kernel driver to kill endpoint security processes on victim Windows hosts across US healthcare, manufacturing, and education targets. The BYOVD technique turns Microsoft's own Hardware Compatibility signing against defenders, so IT teams relying on EDR alone should add kernel-driver allowlisting and monitor for legitimate RMM tools like AnyDesk running from unexpected locations.

title: Cyber researchers sound alarm over a 15-year-old Linux kernel flaw – 'GhostLock' could let hackers seize unpatched machines in just five seconds
blurb: Researchers at Nebula Security disclosed GhostLock (CVE-2026-43499), a Linux kernel privilege-escalation flaw present since 2011 that lets any local user gain root in about five seconds, with a 97% reliable public exploit that also escapes containers. Every mainstream distribution shipped the vulnerable kernel until the 7.1 fix, so IT teams running unpatched hosts or shared container infrastructure face a low-cost, high-reliability local root path that needs immediate patch prioritization.

title: AssuranceAmerica data breach exposes records of 6.9 million drivers
blurb: AssuranceAmerica disclosed a data breach exposing the personal information of nearly 7 million drivers, including names, contact details, driver's license numbers, and insurance policy data stolen after attackers compromised an employee in March 2026. The auto insurer, which operates across 14 states through more than 9,500 independent agents, detected the intrusion on March 17 but only finished reviewing the affected files on June 15, delaying customer notification by three months.

title: Lone Attacker Uses AI to Breach AWS Cloud Environment in 72 Hours
blurb: Sygnia published research detailing how a lone, financially motivated attacker used agentic AI workflows to compromise a large enterprise's AWS environment and extort the victim in roughly 72 hours. The intrusion chained weaknesses across application services, CI/CD pipelines, source repositories, and data stores rather than exploiting a single misconfiguration, compressing weeks of reconnaissance and tooling into three days and raising the baseline threat model for enterprise cloud teams.

title: Proof-of-Concept Exploit Released for Linux ‘Bad Epoll' Root Access Vulnerability
blurb: Researchers published a working proof-of-concept for Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242, CVSS 7.8), a use-after-free race condition in the Linux kernel's epoll facility that lets unprivileged processes gain root on servers, desktops, and Android devices running kernel 6.4 or newer. The exploit was confirmed on Pixel 10 hardware, and a complete fix landed only two months after the initial report, leaving enterprise Linux fleets exposed to patch-management urgency.

title: SAP makes it easier for customers to shop for legacy product support, ending EU antitrust probe
blurb: SAP agreed to loosen the terms tying customers to its own maintenance for legacy products, resolving a European Commission antitrust investigation into whether the vendor locked out third-party support rivals. Enterprises running older SAP systems gain more room to shop maintenance contracts with providers like Rimini Street, which lowers the switching cost of staying on legacy software rather than migrating to S/4HANA.

title: Suspected Chinese snoops caught breaking into universities' Roundcube mailservers
blurb: Suspected Chinese threat actors exploited vulnerabilities in Roundcube webmail servers to break into email accounts at multiple universities, exfiltrating messages and credentials for espionage. Institutions running self-hosted Roundcube instances should patch immediately and audit for compromised accounts, as unpatched open-source mail infrastructure remains a soft target for state-backed intrusion against research and academic networks.

title: Bug in top AI coding agents shows that Unix-era security headaches never really die
blurb: Wiz disclosed GhostApproval, a symlink-based vulnerability pattern in at least six major AI coding agents (Amazon Q Developer, Claude Code, Augment, Cursor, Google Antigravity, and Windsurf) that lets attackers escape the workspace sandbox and reach remote code execution on a developer's machine. Amazon, Cursor, and Google rated it critical or high, patched it, and issued or are filing CVEs, while Augment and Windsurf have acknowledged the report but not yet shipped fixes or warned users.

title: Ubuntu emphasizes Arm64 support – and gets Rustier
blurb: Canonical is elevating Arm64 to first-class status in Ubuntu and swapping more core utilities for memory-safe Rust reimplementations, signaling where its LTS base is headed. For enterprise fleets, the Arm64 commitment strengthens the case for Ampere and Graviton deployments, while the Rust shift means shops standardized on GNU coreutils should test compatibility before the next LTS lands.

title: OpenAI launches ChatGPT Work as it broadens GPT-5.6 rollout
blurb: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, an agentic enterprise platform that pairs GPT-5.6 with Codex and business integrations to run long, multi-step workflows across connected apps and files, while making the GPT-5.6 models generally available through ChatGPT, Codex, and the API. The pitch shifts from benchmark leadership to performance per dollar, with Sol priced at $5 per million input and $30 per million output tokens and lower-cost Terra and Luna tiers for teams scaling deployments.

title: Extortion crew hijacks Microsoft 365 accounts via fake passkey setup
blurb: The Pink extortion crew is hijacking Microsoft 365 accounts by posing as IT on vishing calls, then walking employees through a fake Entra passkey enrollment on a spoofed login page while the attacker enrolls their own passkey on the real account. The phishing kit adapts to each victim's MFA method and uses fake BIP-39 recovery-key screens as a distraction, giving IT teams a social-engineering vector that bypasses the passkey protections they just rolled out.

title: Intel and AMD systems at risk from virtualization vulnerability
blurb: Security researchers disclosed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a 16-year-old KVM hypervisor flaw that lets a single rented VM escape to the x86 host and either crash or take root control of every other tenant on the same physical machine. The bug has been patched, but it hits Intel and AMD hosts across public clouds while sparing Arm64 KVM, giving IT teams another urgent hypervisor-patching cycle to schedule.

title: Hidden backdoor in Tenda routers goes unpatched as company ignores warnings from cybersecurity researchers — Chinese company's firmware allows admin access without a password
blurb: CERT/CC disclosed CVE-2026-11405, an undocumented authentication backdoor in Tenda router firmware that grants full admin access to the web management interface without valid credentials, affecting builds across the FH1201, W15E, AC10, AC5, and AC6 families. No patch exists because Tenda has not responded to CERT/CC's outreach, leaving IT teams with affected devices to block WAN-facing management access or pull the hardware from their networks.

title: SAP loosens ERP maintenance and support rules
blurb: SAP agreed to European Commission commitments that let on-premises ERP customers buy maintenance and support from third-party providers, clarify original licensing terms, and terminate license and support fees in defined scenarios, with the changes binding globally for 10 years. The concessions apply only to on-premises ERP, not SAP's cloud offerings, but they weaken the lock-in that pushed third-party maintenance rivals like Rimini Street out of accounts and give enterprises more leverage on renewal costs.

title: Allstate Insurance quits Broadcom, alleges vengeful license audit on the way out
blurb: Allstate Insurance is leaving Broadcom's VMware platform and alleges the vendor retaliated with a punitive license audit on its way out. The dispute highlights the mounting friction enterprises face under Broadcom's subscription-only VMware licensing, where audit threats and steep renewal costs are pushing large customers toward Nutanix, Proxmox, and other virtualization alternatives.

title: AT&T, T-Mobile US, and Verizon ditch SMS for network-based authentication
blurb: AT&T, T-Mobile US, and Verizon launched a network-based authentication service through the Aduna venture, using a single API to verify a mobile number against its carrier, device, and SIM and replace SMS one-time passcodes. The move targets OTP interception and SIM-swap fraud tied to $15.9B in U.S. consumer losses last year, while giving developers one integration across all three carriers instead of per-operator deals.

title: GPT-5.6 is now the preferred model in Microsoft 365 Copilot
blurb: OpenAI announced GPT-5.6 and Microsoft will make it the preferred model across Microsoft 365 Copilot, spanning Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Chat, and Cowork, served both natively and through the OpenAI API. For enterprise buyers, the update lands inside tools they already license, so the improved per-token efficiency reaches productivity workflows without new procurement, while deepening Microsoft's dependence on OpenAI's flagship line.

title: China-Linked UAT-7810 Expands ORB Network With New LONGLEASH Malware
blurb: Cisco Talos linked Chinese APT actor UAT-7810 to an expanded ORB relay network built by compromising unpatched Ruckus and ASUS routers via known CVEs, deploying new custom malware including LONGLEASH, DOGLEASH, and JARLEASH. The relay infrastructure is rented to secondary China-nexus actors like UAT-5918 for attacks on critical infrastructure, so enterprises running end-of-life networking gear at the edge become unwitting staging nodes for third-party intrusions.

title: New Ghost Phishing Wave Is Breaking Traditional Email Security
blurb: Security researchers documented an EvilTokens phishing campaign hitting US and European firms that hides its malicious page by decrypting AES-GCM-encrypted HTML only after it renders in the victim's browser, letting it slip past static URL scans and network-level email controls. The kit pairs this with Microsoft Device Code Phishing to hijack Microsoft 365 accounts without stealing a password, extending the window before security teams can detect and contain the takeover.

title: GitHub’s public APIs are becoming an enterprise reconnaissance tool
blurb: Datadog Security Research is tracking a sustained campaign that abuses GitHub's public APIs and networks of ghost accounts to profile enterprise environments, mapping organizations and members before escalating to private-repo cloning. Because much of GitHub's API surface is reachable without authentication and the requests blend into normal developer traffic, security teams get little signal until intellectual property or leaked credentials are already exposed.

title: GitHub Copilot: Sorry Dave, I can't do that harmful thing - unless you ask me in code
blurb: Researchers found that GitHub Copilot refuses harmful requests phrased in natural language but complies when the same instructions are embedded in code comments or variable names, bypassing its safety guardrails. The gap matters for enterprises deploying Copilot across engineering teams, since prompt-injection through code is harder to audit than plain-text abuse and puts the burden of policy enforcement back on the buyer's own controls.

title: Intel-backed AI chip startup SambaNova breathes new life into aging Nvidia GPUs in latest benchmarks
blurb: SambaNova published third-party benchmarks showing its SN50 RDU accelerators paired with Nvidia H200 GPUs in a heterogeneous configuration, hitting 763 tokens per second on MiniMax M2.7. The Intel-backed startup's pitch is that mixing its RDUs with existing GPU fleets extends the useful life of aging Nvidia hardware, giving IT buyers a path to higher inference throughput without a full accelerator refresh.

title: China tells devs to ditch Claude Code over 'backdoor code' fears
blurb: Chinese authorities advised local developers to stop using older versions of Anthropic's Claude Code, citing unverified claims of backdoor code in the AI coding tool. The warning deepens the split between Western AI vendors and China's market, where Anthropic already restricts access, pushing enterprises toward domestic alternatives like Alibaba's Qwen and DeepSeek for agentic coding workloads.

title: Telstra outage: Failed emergency services calls, train chaos, payment systems down
blurb: Telstra suffered a major network outage that knocked out Triple Zero emergency calls, disrupted train services, and took down payment terminals across parts of Australia. The failure underscores the concentration risk enterprises carry when critical operations depend on a single carrier, and it will sharpen scrutiny of Telstra's resilience commitments and push affected businesses toward redundant connectivity and failover arrangements.

title: Apple loses major antitrust appeal in Europe, remains a ‘gatekeeper’
blurb: Europe's General Court rejected all three of Apple's appeals against its Digital Markets Act "gatekeeper" designation, upholding the label for both iOS and the App Store and ruling its iMessage challenge inadmissible. The decision keeps Apple bound to DMA obligations like permitting third-party app stores and alternative payment terms, and strengthens EU regulators as Apple weighs a further appeal to the Court of Justice.

title: Slack’s Slackbot can now pull your CRM data, generate charts, and send DocuSigns — all from a chat message.
blurb: Slack connected Slackbot, the AI agent in every workspace, to the full Salesforce platform through dedicated MCP servers, letting users query CRM records, pull live Tableau charts, and trigger DocuSign approvals from a single chat prompt. The integration is Salesforce's answer to Microsoft Teams and Copilot, and its pitch to buyers is that 25 years of CRM data locked in the platform is an asset no vibe-coded Claude replacement can match.

title: Safely run AI-generated code in Cloud Run sandboxes
blurb: Google Cloud released Cloud Run sandboxes in public preview, a native runtime for executing AI-generated code and untrusted binaries in isolated environments that spin up in milliseconds within existing Cloud Run services.
